caption: Reversible cell injury and necrosis. The principal cellular alterations that characterize reversible cell injury and necrosis are illustrated. By conven- tion, reversible injury is considered to culminate in necrosis if the injurious stimulus is not removed.
summary: The image illustrates the progression of cellular injury, specifically showing three stages: a normal cell, a cell undergoing reversible injury, and a cell undergoing necrosis. The normal cell is depicted with intact organelles and a healthy nucleus. The cell in reversible injury shows signs of swelling and organelle damage, such as mitochondrial swelling and ribosome detachment. The necrotic cell exhibits severe damage, including nuclear fragmentation (karyorrhexis), nuclear condensation (pyknosis), and nuclear dissolution (karyolysis), along with cytoplasmic vacuolization and membrane disruption. The caption explains that reversible injury can progress to necrosis if the injurious stimulus persists.

caption: Morphologic changes in reversible and irreversible cell injury (necrosis). (A) Normal kidney tubules with viable epithelial cells.
summary: The image displays normal kidney tubules with viable epithelial cells, characterized by intact cellular structures and nuclei, indicative of healthy tissue without signs of necrosis or other pathological changes.

caption: Morphologic changes in reversible and irreversible cell injury (necrosis). (B) Early (reversible) ischemic injury showing surface blebs, increased eosinophilia of cytoplasm, and swelling of occasional cells.
summary: The image displays early reversible ischemic injury characterized by surface blebs, increased eosinophilia of cytoplasm, and swelling of occasional cells.

caption: Morphologic changes in reversible and irreversible cell injury (necrosis). (C) Necrotic (irreversible) injury of epithelial cells, with loss of nuclei and fragmentation of cells and leakage of contents.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue, specifically showing epithelial cells undergoing necrotic (irreversible) injury. The cells exhibit loss of nuclei and fragmentation, with leakage of intracellular contents into the surrounding space.

caption: Coagulative necrosis. (A) A wedge-shaped kidney infarct (yellow) with preservation of the outlines.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney slice exhibiting a wedge-shaped area of coagulative necrosis, identified as an infarct. The necrotic tissue appears yellow and contrasts with the surrounding viable, reddish-brown renal parenchyma, while maintaining the structural outlines of the tissue.

caption: Coagulative necrosis. (B) Microscopic view of the edge of the infarct, with normal kidney (N) and necrotic cells in the infarct (I). The necrotic cells show preserved outlines with loss of nuclei, and an inflammatory infiltrate is present (difficult to discern at this magnification).
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of kidney tissue, specifically illustrating the edge of an infarct. It contrasts normal kidney tissue with necrotic cells within the infarct area. The necrotic cells are characterized by preserved cellular outlines but a loss of nuclei, accompanied by an inflammatory infiltrate.

caption: Liquefactive necrosis. An infarct in the brain shows dissolution of the tissue.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain section exhibiting liquefactive necrosis. The caption identifies the lesion as an infarct, characterized by the dissolution of the tissue, resulting in a soft, yellowish, and somewhat liquid-like appearance distinct from the surrounding brain parenchyma.

caption: Caseous necrosis. Tuberculosis of the lung, with a large area of caseous necrosis containing yellow-white (cheesy) debris.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung affected by tuberculosis. It features a large, distinct area of caseous necrosis, characterized by a yellow-white, cheesy, and granular appearance, contrasting with the surrounding lung tissue.

caption: Fat necrosis in acute pancreatitis. The areas of white chalky deposits represent foci of fat necrosis with calcium soap formation (saponification) at sites of lipid breakdown in the mesentery.
summary: The image displays a gross pathological specimen of mesenteric fat exhibiting acute pancreatitis-related changes. The tissue shows extensive areas of yellowish-white, chalky deposits which represent foci of fat necrosis with calcium soap formation (saponification) at sites of lipid breakdown.

caption: Fibrinoid necrosis in an artery in a patient with polyarteritis nodosa. The wall of the artery shows a circumferential bright pink area of necrosis with protein deposition and inflammation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an artery exhibiting fibrinoid necrosis, a pathological finding characterized by the deposition of fibrin-like material within the vessel wall. This specific presentation is identified as occurring in a patient with Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN), a systemic necrotizing vasculitis. The visual evidence shows a circumferential bright pink area of necrosis replacing the arterial wall, surrounded by a dense infiltrate of inflammatory cells.

caption: Apoptosis. The cellular alterations in apoptosis are illustrated. Contrast these with the changes that characterize necrotic cell death, shown in Fig. 2.3.
summary: The image illustrates the sequential morphological changes of a cell undergoing apoptosis, a form of programmed cell death. It depicts the progression from a healthy cell to a shrunken cell with condensed chromatin, followed by the formation of membrane blebs, and finally the fragmentation into apoptotic bodies.

caption: Morphologic appearance of apoptotic cells. Apoptotic cells (some indicated by arrows) in colonic epithelium are shown. (Some preparative regimens for colonoscopy may induce apoptosis in epithelial cells, which explains the presence of dead cells in this biopsy.) Note the fragmented nuclei with condensed chromatin and the shrunken cell bodies, some with pieces falling off.
summary: The image displays a histological section of colonic epithelium exhibiting apoptotic cells, characterized by fragmented nuclei with condensed chromatin and shrunken cell bodies, some with cytoplasmic fragments detaching. This morphologic appearance is noted as potentially induced by preparative regimens for colonoscopy.

caption: Physiologic hypertrophy of the uterus during pregnancy. (A) Gross appearance of a normal uterus (right) and a gravid uterus (left) that was removed for postpartum bleeding.
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical comparison of a normal uterus (right) and a gravid uterus (left), illustrating the significant enlargement that occurs during pregnancy. The gravid uterus, which was removed postpartum due to bleeding, shows marked hypertrophy compared to the normal-sized uterus.

caption: Physiologic hypertrophy of the uterus during pregnancy. (B) Small spindle-shaped uterine smooth muscle cells from a normal uterus.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a normal uterus, specifically highlighting the smooth muscle cells. The cells are spindle-shaped and arranged in a fascicular pattern, characteristic of the myometrium. The caption indicates this is a representation of physiologic hypertrophy of the uterus during pregnancy.

caption: Physiologic hypertrophy of the uterus during pregnancy. (C) Large, plump hypertrophied smooth muscle cells from a gravid uterus; compare with B. (B and C, Same magnification.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of a gravid uterus, illustrating physiologic hypertrophy characterized by large, plump smooth muscle cells. The tissue is stained with H&E, showing elongated, eosinophilic fibers with centrally located nuclei, indicative of the adaptive growth of the myometrium during pregnancy.

caption: The relationship among normal, adapted, reversibly injured, and dead myocardial cells. The cellular adaptation depicted here is hypertrophy, the cause of reversible injury is ischemia, and the irreversible injury is ischemic coagulative necrosis. In the example of myocardial hypertrophy (lower left), the left ventricular wall is thicker than 2 cm (normal, 1–1.5 cm). Reversibly injured myocardium shows functional effects without any gross or light microscopic changes, or reversible changes such as cellular swelling and fatty change (shown here). In the specimen showing necrosis (lower right) the transmural light area in the posterolateral left ventricle represents an acute myocardial infarction. All three transverse sections of myocardium were stained with triphenyltetra- zolium chloride, an enzyme substrate that colors viable myocardium magenta. Failure to stain is due to enzyme loss after cell death.
summary: The image illustrates the relationship among normal, adapted (hypertrophied), reversibly injured, and dead (necrotic) myocardial cells. It shows a normal left ventricular myocardium with a wall thickness of 1–1.5 cm, a hypertrophied myocardium with a wall thickness greater than 2 cm, reversibly injured myocardium exhibiting cellular swelling and fatty change, and a necrotic myocardium with an acute myocardial infarction characterized by a transmural light area in the posterolateral left ventricle. All sections are stained with triphenyltetrazolium chloride, which colors viable myocardium magenta, while necrotic areas fail to stain due to enzyme loss.

caption: Lipofuscin granules in cardiac myocytes (deposits indicated by arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological section of cardiac tissue, specifically highlighting cardiac myocytes. The caption identifies the presence of lipofuscin granules within these cells, which are indicated by arrows. These granules appear as small, yellowish-brown deposits scattered throughout the cytoplasm of the muscle fibers.

caption: Hemosiderin granules in liver cells. (A) Hematoxylin-eosin–stained section showing golden-brown, finely granular pigment.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of liver tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). The primary feature is the presence of golden-brown, finely granular pigment deposits within the cytoplasm of the hepatocytes, identified as hemosiderin granules.

caption: Hemosiderin granules in liver cells. (B) Iron deposits shown by a special staining process called the Prussian blue reaction.
summary: The image displays liver cells containing hemosiderin granules, which are iron deposits visualized using the Prussian blue reaction, a special staining technique that highlights these deposits in blue against a pink background.

caption: The multistep process of leukocyte migration through blood vessels, shown here for neutrophils. The leukocytes first roll, then become activated and adhere to endothelium, then transmigrate across the endothelium, pierce the basement membrane, and move toward chemoattractants emanating from the source of injury. Different molecules play predominant roles at each step of this process: selectins in rolling; chemokines (usually displayed bound to proteoglycans) in activating the neutrophils to increase avidity of integrins; integrins in firm adhesion; and CD31 (PECAM-1) in transmigration. ICAM-1, Inter- cellular adhesion molecule-1; PECAM-1 (CD31), platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1; TNF, tumor necrosis factor.
summary: The image illustrates the multistep process of neutrophil migration through blood vessels, including rolling, activation, adhesion, transmigration across the endothelium, piercing the basement membrane, and movement toward chemoattractants. Key molecules involved include selectins, chemokines, integrins, ICAM-1, PECAM-1 (CD31), and TNF.

caption: Serous inflammation. Low-power view of a cross section of a skin blister showing the epidermis separated from the dermis by a focal collection of serous effusion.
summary: The image displays a low-power histological cross-section of a skin blister. It features the epidermis separated from the underlying dermis by a focal collection of serous fluid, indicative of serous inflammation.

caption: Fibrinous pericarditis. (B) A pink meshwork of fibrin exudate (F) overlies the pericardial surface (P).
summary: The image displays a histological section of fibrinous pericarditis, characterized by a pink meshwork of fibrin exudate (F) covering the pericardial surface (P).

caption: Fibrinous pericarditis. (A) Deposits of fibrin on the pericardium.
summary: The image displays a gross pathological specimen of a heart exhibiting fibrinous pericarditis, characterized by the presence of fibrin deposits on the pericardial surface.

caption: Purulent inflammation. (A) Multiple bacterial abscesses (arrows) in the lung in a case of bronchopneumonia.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung affected by bronchopneumonia. The tissue exhibits a mottled, brownish-red appearance with multiple distinct, yellowish-white focal lesions scattered throughout. These lesions are identified as bacterial abscesses, representing areas of purulent inflammation.

caption: Purulent inflammation. (B) The abscess contains neutrophils and cellular debris, and is surrounded by congested blood vessels.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing a large, circumscribed area of purulent inflammation, identified as an abscess. This lesion is characterized by a dense accumulation of neutrophils and cellular debris. The abscess is surrounded by a zone of congested blood vessels, indicating an active inflammatory response.

caption: The morphology of an ulcer. (A) A chronic duodenal ulcer.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a chronic duodenal ulcer, characterized by a distinct crater-like depression in the tissue surface.

caption: The morphology of an ulcer. (B) Low-power cross-section view of a duodenal ulcer crater with an acute inflamma- tory exudate in the base.
summary: The image displays a low-power cross-section view of a duodenal ulcer crater, characterized by an acute inflammatory exudate located at the base of the ulcer.

caption: A focus of inflammation containing numerous eosinophils.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing a focal area of inflammation characterized by a dense infiltration of eosinophils, identifiable by their bilobed nuclei and prominent eosinophilic (pink/red) cytoplasmic granules.

caption: Typical tuberculous granuloma showing an area of central necrosis surrounded by multiple multinucleate giant cells, epithelioid cells, and lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a tuberculous granuloma, characterized by a central area of necrosis surrounded by a rim of multinucleate giant cells, epithelioid cells, and lymphocytes.

caption: (A) Granulation tissue showing numerous blood vessels, edema, and a loose extracellular matrix containing occasional inflammatory cells. Collagen is stained blue by the trichrome stain; minimal mature collagen can be seen at this point.
summary: The image displays granulation tissue characterized by numerous blood vessels, edema, a loose extracellular matrix with occasional inflammatory cells, and minimal mature collagen stained blue by trichrome stain.

caption: (B) Trichrome stain of mature scar, showing dense collagen (stained blue) and scattered vascular channels.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a mature scar stained with Trichrome stain, highlighting dense collagen fibers in blue and scattered vascular channels in red.

caption: Sequential conversion of factor X to factor Xa by way of the extrinsic pathway, followed by conversion of factor II (prothrombin) to factor IIa (throm- bin). The initial reaction complex consists of a protease (factor VIIa), a substrate (factor X), and a reaction accelerator (tissue factor) assembled on a platelet phospholipid surface. Calcium ions hold the assembled components together and are essential for the reaction. Activated factor Xa then becomes the protease component of the next complex in the cascade, converting prothrombin to thrombin (factor IIa) in the presence of a different reaction accelerator, factor Va.
summary: The image illustrates the sequential conversion of coagulation factors in the extrinsic pathway: factor VIIa activates factor X to factor Xa on a platelet phospholipid surface with tissue factor and calcium ions; subsequently, factor Xa converts prothrombin (factor II) to thrombin (factor IIa) using factor Va as an accelerator.

caption: An organized thrombus. Low-power view of a thrombosed artery stained for elastic tissue. The original lumen is delineated by the inter- nal elastic lamina (arrows) and is completely filled with organized thrombus.
summary: The image displays a low-power histological view of an artery that has undergone thrombosis. The vessel lumen is completely occluded by an organized thrombus. The original lumen is identified by the internal elastic lamina, which is highlighted by elastic tissue staining (likely Verhoeff-Van Gieson stain).

caption: Embolus derived from a lower-extremity deep venous thrombus lodged in a pulmonary artery branch.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a pulmonary artery branch containing an embolus, which originated from a deep venous thrombus in the lower extremity. The embolus is lodged within the vessel, obstructing blood flow.

caption: Remote kidney infarct, now replaced by a large fibrotic scar.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting a remote infarct, characterized by a large, pale, fibrotic scar replacing the previously necrotic tissue.

caption: The human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex and the structure of HLA molecules. (A) The location of genes in the HLA complex. The relative locations, sizes, and distances between genes are not to scale. Genes that encode several proteins involved in antigen processing (the TAP transporter, com- ponents of the proteasome, and HLA-DM) are located in the class II region (not shown).
summary: The image and caption describe the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex, highlighting the genomic organization of HLA genes and their role in antigen processing. The figure illustrates the 3D structure of HLA molecules, showing class I (blue) and class II (teal) domains with associated peptide-binding regions (yellow/green), alongside a schematic of gene locations within the HLA complex.

caption: The human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex and the structure of HLA molecules. (B) Schematic diagrams and crystal structures of class I and class II HLA molecules. (Crystal structures are courtesy of Dr. P. Bjorkman, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California.)
summary: The image displays a schematic representation of the human leukocyte antigen (HLA) complex, specifically illustrating the structural differences between HLA class I and class II molecules. The diagram highlights the alpha helices and beta sheets that form the peptide-binding groove, with class I molecules showing a single alpha helix and class II molecules displaying two alpha helices. The image also includes a crystal structure of an HLA molecule, providing a three-dimensional view of the protein's conformation.

caption: Libman-Sacks endocarditis of the mitral valve in lupus erythema- tosus. The vegetations attached to the margin of the thickened valve leaflet are indicated by arrows.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart with Libman-Sacks endocarditis affecting the mitral valve. The vegetations, characteristic of this condition, are attached to the margin of the thickened valve leaflet and are indicated by arrows.

caption: Sjögren syndrome. (B) Intense lymphocytic and plasma cell infiltration with ductal epithelial hyperplasia in a salivary gland. (A, Courtesy of Dr. Richard Sontheimer, Department of Dermatology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas. B, Courtesy of Dr. Dennis Burns, Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of a salivary gland affected by Sjögren syndrome, characterized by intense lymphocytic and plasma cell infiltration and ductal epithelial hyperplasia.

caption: Sjögren syndrome. (A) Enlargement of the salivary gland.
summary: The image depicts a clinical presentation of Sjögren syndrome, characterized by visible enlargement of the salivary gland in the neck region of a patient.

caption: Acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) involving the skin. (A) Patchy epithelial cell death and dermal infiltrates of mononuclear cells (lymphocytes and macrophages).
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin exhibiting features of acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). Key pathological findings include patchy epithelial cell death (apoptosis) within the epidermis and a dermal infiltrate composed of mononuclear cells, specifically lymphocytes and macrophages.

caption: Acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) involving the skin. (B) Focally dead epithelial cells (arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin exhibiting features of acute graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). The epidermis shows significant damage, characterized by the presence of necrotic keratinocytes (dead epithelial cells) indicated by arrows, which is a hallmark of this condition.

caption: Amyloidosis. (B) Note the yellow-green birefringence of the deposits when observed by a polarizing microscope. (B, Courtesy of Dr. Trace Worrell and Sandy Hinton, Depart- ment of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue exhibiting amyloidosis, characterized by extracellular deposits that show yellow-green birefringence under polarized light, a hallmark feature of amyloid when stained with Congo red.
entities: University of Texas Southwestern Medical School

caption: Amyloidosis. (A) A section of liver stained with Congo red reveals pink-red deposits of amyloid in the walls of blood vessels and along sinusoids.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue stained with Congo red, revealing characteristic pink-red deposits of amyloid material within the walls of blood vessels and along sinusoids, indicative of hepatic amyloidosis.

caption: Colonic polyp. This glandular tumor is seen projecting into the colonic lumen. The polyp is attached to the mucosa by a distinct stalk.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a colonic polyp, characterized by a glandular tumor projecting into the colonic lumen and attached to the underlying mucosa by a distinct stalk.

caption: Mixed tumor of the parotid gland. Small nests of epithelial cells and myxoid stroma forming cartilage and bone (an unusual feature) are present in this field.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a mixed tumor of the parotid gland. The field reveals small nests of epithelial cells embedded within a myxoid stroma. An unusual feature is the presence of cartilage and bone formation within the tumor.

caption: Pleomorphic malignant tumor (rhabdomyosarcoma). Note the marked variation in cell and nuclear sizes, the hyperchromatic nuclei, and the presence of tumor giant cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pleomorphic malignant tumor identified as rhabdomyosarcoma. Key features include significant variation in cell and nuclear sizes, the presence of hyperchromatic nuclei, and distinct tumor giant cells.

caption: Pleomorphic malignant tumor (rhabdomyosarcoma). Note the marked variation in cell and nuclear sizes, the hyperchromatic nuclei, and the presence of tumor giant cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pleomorphic malignant tumor identified as rhabdomyosarcoma. Key features include significant variation in cell and nuclear sizes, the presence of hyperchromatic nuclei, and distinct tumor giant cells.

caption: High-power detailed view of anaplastic tumor cells shows cellular and nuclear variation in size and shape. The prominent cell in the center field has an abnormal tripolar spindle.
summary: The image displays a high-power microscopic view of an anaplastic tumor, characterized by significant cellular and nuclear pleomorphism (variation in size and shape). A prominent feature is a central cell exhibiting an abnormal tripolar spindle, indicative of the disorganized growth typical of malignancy.

caption: Carcinoma in situ. (A) Low-power view shows that the entire thickness of the epithelium is replaced by atypical dysplastic cells. There is no orderly differentiation of squamous cells. The basement membrane is intact, and there is no tumor in the subepithelial stroma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue demonstrating carcinoma in situ. At low magnification, the entire thickness of the epithelium is replaced by atypical dysplastic cells, lacking orderly differentiation. The basement membrane remains intact, and there is no invasion into the subepithelial stroma.

caption: Carcinoma in situ. (B) High-power view of another region shows failure of normal differentiation, marked nuclear and cellular pleomorphism, and numerous mitotic figures extending toward the surface. The intact basement membrane (below) is not seen in this section.
summary: The image displays a high-power histological view of a region exhibiting carcinoma in situ. Key features include a lack of normal cellular differentiation, significant nuclear and cellular pleomorphism, and the presence of numerous mitotic figures extending towards the surface. The caption notes that the intact basement membrane is not visible in this specific section.

caption: Fibroadenoma of the breast. The tan-colored, encapsulated small tumor is sharply demarcated from the whiter breast tissue.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a breast fibroadenoma. It shows a distinct, tan-colored, encapsulated mass that is sharply demarcated from the surrounding whiter breast tissue.

caption: Microscopic view of fibroadenoma of the breast seen in Fig. 6.7. The fibrous capsule (right) sharply delimits the tumor from the surrounding tissue.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a fibroadenoma of the breast, characterized by a distinct fibrous capsule on the right side that sharply separates the tumor from the surrounding tissue.

caption: Cut section of invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast. The lesion is retracted, infiltrating the surrounding breast substance, and was stony-hard on palpation.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a breast lesion, identified as invasive ductal carcinoma. The cut section reveals a retracted, firm mass with a yellowish-tan appearance, infiltrating the surrounding breast tissue. A ruler is present for scale.

caption: Cut section of invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast. The lesion is retracted, infiltrating the surrounding breast substance, and was stony-hard on palpation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a breast lesion identified as invasive ductal carcinoma. The tissue exhibits a retracted, infiltrative growth pattern into the surrounding breast substance, characterized by a solid, purple-stained mass with irregular borders.

caption: A liver studded with metastatic cancer.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver exhibiting multiple, distinct, pale, round nodules scattered throughout the parenchyma. These nodules are identified as metastatic cancer deposits, indicating that the primary tumor originated elsewhere in the body and spread to the liver.

caption: Comparison between a benign tumor of the myometrium (leiomyoma) and a malignant tumor of similar origin (leiomyosarcoma).
summary: The image illustrates a comparison between a benign tumor of the myometrium (leiomyoma) and a malignant tumor of similar origin (leiomyosarcoma). The benign tumor is depicted as a well-circumscribed, smooth, and uniform mass, while the malignant tumor shows irregular, infiltrative growth with necrotic areas.

caption: The metastatic cascade: The sequential steps involved in the hematogenous spread of a tumor.
summary: The image illustrates the metastatic cascade, depicting the sequential steps of hematogenous spread of a tumor. It shows a tumor cell detaching from a primary tumor, entering the bloodstream, traveling through a blood vessel, and eventually extravasating to form a secondary tumor in a distant organ.

caption: Diverse tumor types that share a common mutation, BRAF (V600E), may be candidates for treatment with the same BRAF inhibitors.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue, likely a tumor, characterized by a complex, branching, papillary-like architecture with cellular proliferation. The caption indicates that this image represents a diverse tumor type sharing the BRAF (V600E) mutation, which is a target for specific inhibitor therapies.

caption: Diverse tumor types that share a common mutation, BRAF (V600E), may be candidates for treatment with the same BRAF inhibitors.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a tumor, likely stained with H&E, showing cellular morphology. The caption indicates that this figure illustrates diverse tumor types that share the BRAF (V600E) mutation, suggesting they are candidates for treatment with BRAF inhibitors.

caption: Diverse tumor types that share a common mutation, BRAF (V600E), may be candidates for treatment with the same BRAF inhibitors.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of cells, likely from a blood smear or bone marrow aspirate, featuring several large, blue-stained cells with prominent nuclei surrounded by numerous smaller, pale red blood cells. The caption indicates that these cells represent diverse tumor types sharing a common BRAF (V600E) mutation, suggesting they are candidates for treatment with BRAF inhibitors.

caption: Mild to moderate changes of CF in the pancreas. The ducts are dilated and plugged with eosinophilic mucin, and the parenchymal glands are atrophic and replaced by fibrous tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pancreas affected by Cystic Fibrosis (CF). The caption describes mild to moderate pathological changes, specifically noting the dilation of ducts which are plugged with eosinophilic mucin, and the atrophy of parenchymal glands accompanied by replacement with fibrous tissue.

caption: Lungs of a patient who died of cystic fibrosis. Extensive mucous plugging and dilation of the tracheobronchial tree are apparent. The pul- monary parenchyma is consolidated by a combination of both secretions and pneumonia; the greenish discoloration is the product of Pseudomo- nas infections.
summary: The image displays the lungs of a patient who died of cystic fibrosis, showing extensive mucous plugging and dilation of the tracheobronchial tree. The pulmonary parenchyma is consolidated by a combination of secretions and pneumonia, with greenish discoloration indicative of Pseudomonas infections.

caption: Ganglion cells in Tay-Sachs disease. (B) A portion of a neuron under the electron microscope shows prominent lysosomes with whorled configurations just below part of the nucleus. (A, Courtesy of Dr. Arthur Weinberg, Depart- ment of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, Texas. B, Courtesy of Dr. Joe Rutledge, Children’s Regional Medical Center, Seattle, Washington.)
summary: The image displays histological and electron microscopic features of Tay-Sachs disease, including enlarged ganglion cells with prominent lysosomes exhibiting whorled configurations beneath the nucleus, characteristic of the lipid storage disorder.

caption: Ganglion cells in Tay-Sachs disease. (A) Under the light microscope, a large neuron has obvious lipid vacuolation.
summary: The image displays ganglion cells affected by Tay-Sachs disease, characterized by lipid vacuolation visible under light microscopy.

caption: Niemann-Pick disease in liver. The hepatocytes and Kupffer cells have a foamy, vacuolated appearance resulting from deposition of lipids.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue affected by Niemann-Pick disease. The caption describes the characteristic foamy, vacuolated appearance of the hepatocytes and Kupffer cells, which is caused by the intracellular deposition of lipids.

caption: Gaucher disease involving the bone marrow. (A) Gaucher cells with abundant lipid-laden “wrinkled” cytoplasm.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of bone marrow affected by Gaucher disease, characterized by the presence of Gaucher cells. These cells are notable for their abundant cytoplasm containing lipid droplets, giving them a distinct 'wrinkled' or 'crumpled tissue paper' appearance. The background shows other marrow elements including hematopoietic cells and red blood cells.

caption: Gaucher disease involving the bone marrow. (B) Electron micrograph of Gaucher cells with elongated distended lysosomes.
summary: The image is an electron micrograph showing a Gaucher cell from bone marrow involved in Gaucher disease, characterized by elongated distended lysosomes.

caption: G-banded karyotype from a normal male (46,XY). Also shown is the banding pattern of the X-chromosome with nomenclature of arms, regions, bands, and subbands. (Karyotype courtesy of Dr. Stuart Schwartz, Department of Pathology, University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: The image displays a G-banded karyotype of a normal male with 46 chromosomes (46,XY), alongside a detailed illustration of the X-chromosome banding pattern, including nomenclature for arms, regions, bands, and subbands.

caption: Examples of malformations. Malformations can range in severity from the incidental to the lethal. (C) Stillbirth associated with a lethal malformation, in which the midface structures are fused or ill-formed; in almost all cases, this degree of external dysmorphogenesis is associated with severe internal anomalies such as maldevelopment of the brain and cardiac defects. (A and C, Courtesy of Dr. Reade Quinton, Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, Texas. B, Courtesy of Dr. Beverly Rogers, Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image depicts a stillborn infant with a lethal midface malformation characterized by fused or ill-formed facial structures, indicative of severe dysmorphogenesis. This condition is associated with profound internal anomalies, including maldevelopment of the brain and cardiac defects, underscoring the lethal nature of such malformations.

caption: Examples of malformations. Malformations can range in severity from the incidental to the lethal. (A) Polydactyly (one or more extra digits) and syndactyly (fusion of digits) have little functional consequence when they occur in isolation.
summary: The image displays a newborn with severe craniofacial malformations, including a prominent forehead, flattened nasal bridge, and possible midline facial defects. The caption references examples of malformations such as polydactyly and syndactyly, though the image itself appears to show more profound structural anomalies rather than isolated digit abnormalities.

caption: Examples of malformations. Malformations can range in severity from the incidental to the lethal. (B) Similarly, cleft lip, with or without associated cleft palate, is compatible with life when it occurs as an isolated anomaly; in this case, however, the child had an underlying malformation syndrome (trisomy 13) and expired because of severe cardiac defects.
summary: The image displays a newborn with severe congenital malformations, specifically polydactyly (extra digits) and syndactyly (fused digits), as part of a malformation syndrome. The caption indicates that while isolated cleft lip/palate is compatible with life, this case involved trisomy 13, which led to fatal cardiac defects.

caption: Disruption due to amniotic bands. In the specimen shown, the placenta is at the right, and the band of amnion extends from the top portion of the amniotic sac to encircle the leg of the fetus.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a fetus exhibiting disruption caused by amniotic bands. A band of amnion is seen extending from the top of the amniotic sac and encircling the leg of the fetus, resulting in constriction and tissue damage. The placenta is visible at the right side of the specimen.

caption: Hyaline membrane disease (hematoxylin-eosin stain). Alternating atelectasis and dilation of the alveoli can be seen. Note the eosinophilic thick hyaline membranes lining the dilated alveoli.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E), illustrating the pathological features of Hyaline Membrane Disease. The micrograph reveals a dense infiltration of inflammatory cells within the alveolar spaces, alongside areas of alveolar collapse (atelectasis) and dilation. A key diagnostic feature visible is the presence of eosinophilic, hyaline membranes lining the walls of the dilated alveoli.

caption: Necrotizing enterocolitis. (A) At postmortem examination in a severe case, the entire small bowel was markedly distended and perilously thin (usually this appearance implies impending perforation).
summary: The image depicts a postmortem examination of a severe case of necrotizing enterocolitis, showing the entire small bowel markedly distended and perilously thin, indicating impending perforation.

caption: Necrotizing enterocolitis. (B) The congested portion of the ileum corresponds to areas of hemorrhagic infarction and transmural necrosis. Submucosal gas bubbles (pneumatosis intestinalis) can be seen in several areas (arrows).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the ileum affected by necrotizing enterocolitis. The specimen shows a segment of bowel with areas of congestion, hemorrhagic infarction, and transmural necrosis. Submucosal gas bubbles, indicative of pneumatosis intestinalis, are visible in several areas, marked by arrows.

caption: Hydrops fetalis. (A) Generalized accumulation of fluid in the fetus.
summary: The image depicts a fetus with hydrops fetalis, characterized by generalized fluid accumulation in the fetal tissues, leading to significant swelling and distension of the body.

caption: Hydrops fetalis. (B) Fluid accumulation particularly prominent in the soft tissues of the neck. This condition has been termed cystic hygroma. Cystic hygromas are characteristically seen with, but not limited to, constitutional chromosomal anomalies such as 45,X karyotypes.
summary: The image depicts a fetus with hydrops fetalis, characterized by significant fluid accumulation in the soft tissues of the neck, a condition known as cystic hygroma. This condition is often associated with constitutional chromosomal anomalies, such as 45,X karyotypes.

caption: Bone marrow from an infant infected with parvovirus B19. The arrows point to two erythroid precursors with large homogeneous intra- nuclear inclusions and a surrounding peripheral rim of residual chromatin.
summary: The image displays a bone marrow sample from an infant infected with parvovirus B19, highlighting two erythroid precursors characterized by large, homogeneous intranuclear inclusions and a surrounding peripheral rim of residual chromatin.

caption: Numerous islands of extramedullary hematopoiesis (small blue cells) are scattered among mature hepatocytes in this histologic preparation from an infant with nonimmune hydrops fetalis.
summary: The image displays a histologic section of liver tissue from an infant with nonimmune hydrops fetalis. The preparation reveals numerous islands of extramedullary hematopoiesis, characterized by clusters of small blue cells, scattered among the background of mature hepatocytes.

caption: Kernicterus. Severe hyperbilirubinemia in the neonatal period— for example, secondary to immune hydrolysis—results in deposition of bili- rubin pigment (arrows) in the brain parenchyma. This occurs because the blood–brain barrier is less developed in the neonatal period than it is in adulthood. Infants who survive develop long-term neurologic sequelae.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain affected by kernicterus, a condition resulting from severe hyperbilirubinemia in neonates. The brain parenchyma shows yellowish discoloration due to the deposition of bilirubin pigment, indicated by arrows, which occurs because the blood-brain barrier is less developed in neonates. This condition can lead to long-term neurologic sequelae in surviving infants.

caption: Sacrococcygeal teratoma. Note the size of the lesion compared with that of the infant.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a fetus with a large sacrococcygeal teratoma. The caption highlights the significant size of the tumor relative to the infant, illustrating the mass effect of the lesion.

caption: Retinoblastoma. (A) Poorly cohesive tumor in retina is seen abutting the optic nerve.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an eye affected by retinoblastoma. It shows a poorly cohesive tumor mass located within the retina, which is seen abutting the optic nerve.

caption: Retinoblastoma. (B) Higher-power view showing Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes (arrow) and numerous mitotic figures.
summary: The image displays a high-power microscopic view of a Retinoblastoma tumor, characterized by the presence of Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes and numerous mitotic figures.

caption: Wilms tumor in the lower pole of the kidney with the character- istic tan to gray color and well-circumscribed margins.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a Wilms tumor located in the lower pole of a kidney. The tumor is characterized by its well-circumscribed margins and a tan to gray coloration.

caption: (A) Wilms tumor with tightly packed blue cells consistent with the blastemal component and interspersed primitive tubules, representing the epithelial component.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Wilms tumor, characterized by a densely packed population of small, dark blue cells identified as the blastemal component. Interspersed within this cellular mass are primitive tubular structures, representing the epithelial component of the tumor.

caption: (B) Focal anaplasia was present in other areas within this Wilms tumor, characterized by cells with hyperchromatic, pleomorphic nuclei, and an abnormal mitosis (center of field). Predominance of blastemal morphology and diffuse anaplasia are associated with specific molecular lesions (see text).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Wilms tumor, highlighting focal anaplasia characterized by cells with hyperchromatic, pleomorphic nuclei and an abnormal mitosis. The predominant morphology is blastemal.

caption: FISH. (A) Interphase nucleus from a male patient with suspected trisomy 18. Three different fluorescent probes have been used in a “FISH cocktail”; the green probe hybridizes to the X chromosome centromere (one copy), the red probe to the Y chromosome centromere (one copy), and the aqua probe to the chromosome 18 centromere (three copies).
summary: The image displays an interphase nucleus from a male patient with suspected trisomy 18, visualized using fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) with a cocktail of three fluorescent probes. The green probe targets the X chromosome centromere (one copy), the red probe targets the Y chromosome centromere (one copy), and the aqua probe targets the chromosome 18 centromere (three copies), confirming the diagnosis of trisomy 18.

caption: FISH. (B) A metaphase spread in which two fluorescent probes have been used, one hybridizing to chromosome region 22q13 (green) and the other hybridizing to chromosome region 22q11.2 (red). There are two 22q13 signals. One of the two chromosomes does not stain with the probe for 22q11.2, indicating a microdeletion in this region. This abnormality gives rise to the 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge syndrome).
summary: The image shows a metaphase spread from a FISH analysis using two fluorescent probes: one targeting chromosome region 22q13 (green) and the other targeting 22q11.2 (red). Two 22q13 signals are present, but only one chromosome stains with the 22q11.2 probe, indicating a microdeletion in the 22q11.2 region. This deletion is associated with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, also known as DiGeorge syndrome.

caption: Analysis of copy number variation via SNP cytogenomic array. Genomic DNA is labeled and hybridized to an array containing potentially millions of probe spots. Copy number is determined by overall intensity and genotype is determined by allelic ratio. The example shown is the p arm of chromosome 12 in a pediatric leukemia. Here, the normal areas (green) show neutral (diploid) DNA content and the zygosity plot shows the expected ratio of AA, AB, and BB SNP genotypes. The anomalous area (red) shows decreased overall intensity, and the zygosity plot shows loss of heterozygosity (the AB genotype); together, these findings indicate the presence of a deletion on one copy of chromosome 12p. (Modified from Paulsson K, et al: Genetic landscape of high hyper- diploid childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia, PNAS 107[50]:21719–21724, 2010.)
summary: The image displays a SNP cytogenomic array analysis of the p arm of chromosome 12 in a pediatric leukemia sample. The array shows regions of normal diploid DNA content (green) and an area of decreased intensity with loss of heterozygosity (red), indicating a deletion on one copy of chromosome 12p.

caption: Lead poisoning. Impaired remodeling of calcified cartilage in the epiphyses (arrows) of the wrist has caused a marked increase in their radiodensity, so that they are as radiopaque as the cortical bone.
summary: The image is an X-ray of a wrist showing lead poisoning, characterized by impaired remodeling of calcified cartilage in the epiphyses, leading to increased radiodensity comparable to cortical bone.

caption: (B) Contusion resulting from blunt trauma. The skin is intact, but hemorrhage of subcutaneous vessels has produced extensive discoloration. (A and B, From the teaching collection of the Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image depicts a contusion resulting from blunt trauma, characterized by intact skin with extensive subcutaneous hemorrhage causing discoloration. The visual includes a ruler for scale and highlights the pathological finding of bruising without skin disruption.
entities: University of Texas Southwestern Medical School

caption: (A) Laceration of the scalp: The bridging strands of fibrous tissues are evident.
summary: The image displays a laceration on the scalp with visible bridging strands of fibrous tissue, indicating healing or scarring.

caption: Vascular changes and fibrosis of salivary glands produced by radiation therapy of the neck region. (C) fibrosis and vascular changes consisting of fibrointimal thickening and arteriolar sclerosis. V, Vessel lumen; I, thickened intima. (A–C, Courtesy of Dr. Melissa Upton, Department of Pathology, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington.)
summary: The image displays histological sections of salivary glands exhibiting pathological changes resulting from radiation therapy to the neck region. The primary findings include extensive fibrosis and vascular alterations, specifically characterized by fibrointimal thickening and arteriolar sclerosis, which lead to the narrowing of vessel lumens.

caption: Vascular changes and fibrosis of salivary glands produced by radiation therapy of the neck region. (B) fibrosis caused by radiation;
summary: The image displays a histological section of a salivary gland exhibiting significant pathological changes. The tissue shows extensive fibrosis, characterized by dense connective tissue proliferation, and vascular changes, likely including atrophy of the acinar elements and hyalinization, which are consistent with the effects of radiation therapy on the neck region.

caption: Vascular changes and fibrosis of salivary glands produced by radiation therapy of the neck region. (A) Normal salivary gland;
summary: The image displays a histological section of a salivary gland, illustrating the effects of radiation therapy on the neck region. It highlights vascular changes and fibrosis, contrasting these pathological findings with a normal salivary gland structure.

caption: Childhood malnutrition. (A) Marasmus. Note the loss of muscle mass and subcutaneous fat; the head appears to be too large for the emaciated body.
summary: The image depicts a child suffering from severe malnutrition, specifically marasmus, characterized by significant loss of muscle mass and subcutaneous fat, resulting in an emaciated appearance where the head appears disproportionately large compared to the body.

caption: Childhood malnutrition. (B) Kwashiorkor. The infant shows generalized edema, seen as ascites and puffiness of the face, hands, and legs. (A, From Clinic Barak, Reisebericht Kenya.)
summary: The image depicts an infant suffering from Kwashiorkor, a severe form of childhood malnutrition characterized by generalized edema. Visible signs include ascites (abdominal swelling), puffiness of the face, hands, and legs, indicative of fluid retention due to protein deficiency.

caption: Rickets. (B) Rachitic costochondral junction in which the palisade of cartilage is absent. Darker trabeculae are well-formed bone; paler trabeculae consist of uncalcified osteoid.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a rachitic costochondral junction, characteristic of Rickets. It features a distinct absence of the normal palisade of cartilage. The section shows trabeculae of bone, where darker stained areas represent well-formed bone and paler stained areas represent uncalcified osteoid.

caption: Rickets. (C) Note bowing of legs as a consequence of the formation of poorly mineralized bone in a child with rickets. (B, Courtesy of Dr. Andrew E. Rosenberg, Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, Massachusetts.)
summary: The image depicts a child with rickets, characterized by the bowing of the legs due to poorly mineralized bone formation. The child is shown from the back, highlighting the deformity in the lower limbs.

caption: Rickets. (A) Normal costochondral junction of a young child. Note cartilage palisade formation and orderly transition from cartilage to new bone.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a normal costochondral junction from a young child, stained with H&E. It illustrates the orderly transition from cartilage to new bone, characterized by a distinct cartilage palisade formation at the growth plate.

caption: Examples of viral inclusions. (A) Cytomegalovirus infection in the lung. Infected cells show distinct nuclear (long arrow) and ill-defined cytoplasmic (short arrows) inclusions.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue infected with Cytomegalovirus (CMV). The caption identifies distinct viral inclusions within the infected cells, specifically highlighting a prominent nuclear inclusion (indicated by a long arrow) and less distinct cytoplasmic inclusions (indicated by short arrows).

caption: Examples of viral inclusions. (B) Varicella-zoster virus infection in the skin. Herpes simplex virus and varicella-zoster virus both cause characteristic cytopathologic changes, including fusion of epithelial cells, which produces multinucleate cells with molding of nuclei to one another (long arrow), and eosinophilic haloed nuclear inclusions (short arrow).
summary: The image shows a histological section of skin infected with Varicella-zoster virus (VZV), demonstrating characteristic cytopathologic changes including multinucleate giant cells with nuclear molding and eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions, which are hallmarks of herpesvirus infections.

caption: Examples of viral inclusions. (C) Hepatitis B viral infection in liver. In chronic infections, infected hepatocytes show diffuse granular (“ground-glass”) cyto- plasm, reflecting accumulated hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue from a patient with chronic Hepatitis B viral infection. The infected hepatocytes exhibit diffuse granular cytoplasm, commonly referred to as 'ground-glass' appearance, which is indicative of the accumulation of hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg).

caption: Bacterial morphologies. The bacteria are indicated by arrows. (A) Gram stain preparation of sputum from a patient with pneumonia. Gram-positive, elongated cocci in pairs and short chains (Streptococcus pneumoniae) and a neutrophil are evident.
summary: The image displays a Gram-stained sputum sample from a patient with pneumonia, revealing Gram-positive, elongated cocci arranged in pairs and short chains, characteristic of Streptococcus pneumoniae, alongside a neutrophil.

caption: Bacterial morphologies. The bacteria are indicated by arrows. (B) Gram stain preparation of a bronchoalveolar lavage specimen showing gram-negative intracellular rods typical of members of Enterobacteriaceae such as Klebsiella pneumoniae or Escherichia coli.
summary: The image displays a Gram-stained bronchoalveolar lavage specimen revealing gram-negative intracellular rods, characteristic of Enterobacteriaceae such as Klebsiella pneumoniae or Escherichia coli.

caption: Meningeal blood vessels with angioinvasive Mucor species. Note the irregular width and near right-angle branching of the hyphae (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of meningeal blood vessels invaded by Mucor species. The hyphae exhibit irregular width and characteristic near right-angle branching, which are key diagnostic features highlighted by an arrow.

caption: Coiled Trichinella spiralis larva within a skeletal muscle cell.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skeletal muscle tissue stained with H&E, revealing a cross-section of a skeletal muscle cell containing a coiled Trichinella spiralis larva.

caption: Mononuclear and granulomatous inflammation. (C) Granulomatous inflammation in response to tuberculosis. Note the zone of caseation (asterisk), which normally forms the center of the granuloma, with a surrounding rim of activated epithelioid macrophages, some of which have fused to form giant cells (arrows); this in turn is surrounded by a zone of activated T lymphocytes. This high-magnification view highlights the histologic features; the granulomatous response typically takes the form of a three-dimensional sphere with the offending organism in the central area.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing granulomatous inflammation characteristic of tuberculosis, featuring a central zone of caseation necrosis surrounded by activated epithelioid macrophages, multinucleated giant cells, and a peripheral rim of T lymphocytes.

caption: Mononuclear and granulomatous inflammation. (B) Secondary syphilis in the dermis with perivascular lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and endothelial proliferation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin tissue exhibiting mononuclear and granulomatous inflammation. Specifically, it illustrates the dermal changes associated with secondary syphilis, characterized by a perivascular lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and endothelial proliferation.

caption: Mononuclear and granulomatous inflammation. (A) Acute viral hepatitis characterized by a predominantly lymphocytic infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting mononuclear and granulomatous inflammation. The caption identifies this as acute viral hepatitis, characterized by a predominantly lymphocytic infiltrate.

caption: Schistosoma haematobium infection of the bladder with numerous calcified eggs and extensive scarring.
summary: The image displays a histological section of bladder tissue affected by Schistosoma haematobium infection. It features numerous calcified eggs, which are characteristic of the parasite, embedded within the tissue. The tissue also exhibits extensive scarring, indicating a chronic inflammatory response to the infection.

caption: In the absence of appropriate T-cell–mediated immunity, granulo- matous host response does not occur. Mycobacterium avium infection in a duodenal biopsy from a patient with AIDS, showing massive intracellular macrophage infection with acid-fast organisms (filamentous and pink in this acid-fast stain preparation). The intracellular bacteria persist and even pro- liferate within macrophages, because there are inadequate T cells to mount a granulomatous response. AIDS, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome.
summary: The image displays a duodenal biopsy from an AIDS patient infected with Mycobacterium avium. The acid-fast stain reveals massive intracellular macrophage infection with filamentous, pink-staining organisms. Due to the absence of appropriate T-cell-mediated immunity, no granulomatous host response occurs, allowing the bacteria to persist and proliferate within macrophages.

caption: Stereotypical response to vascular injury. Schematic diagram of intimal thickening, emphasizing intimal smooth muscle cell migration and prolifera- tion associated with extracellular matrix synthesis. Intimal smooth muscle cells may derive from the underlying media or may be recruited from circulating precursors; they are depicted in a color different from that of the medial smooth muscle cells, to emphasize their distinct phenotype.
summary: The image is a schematic diagram illustrating the stereotypical response to vascular injury, specifically focusing on intimal thickening. It depicts the migration and proliferation of intimal smooth muscle cells (shown in pink) from the underlying media or recruited from circulating precursors, along with the synthesis of extracellular matrix (shown in yellow). The diagram also highlights the distinct phenotype of intimal smooth muscle cells compared to medial smooth muscle cells.

caption: The basic structure of an atheromatous plaque.
summary: The image illustrates the basic structure of an atheromatous plaque, a key component of atherosclerosis. It depicts a cross-section of an artery wall showing the layers involved, including the endothelium, intima, media, and adventitia. The plaque itself is shown as a raised lesion within the intima, composed of a lipid core (yellow area) containing cholesterol crystals and foam cells, surrounded by a fibrous cap made of smooth muscle cells and collagen.

caption: Fatty streaks. (A) Aorta with fatty streaks (arrows), mainly near the ostia of branch vessels.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an aorta exhibiting multiple small, yellowish lesions identified as fatty streaks. These lesions are primarily located near the ostia of branch vessels, appearing as subtle discolorations on the vessel wall surface.

caption: Fatty streaks. (B) Fatty streak in an experimental hypercholesterolemic rabbit, demonstrating intimal, macrophage-derived foam cells (arrows). (B, Courtesy of Myron I. Cybulsky, MD, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, Canada.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of an experimental hypercholesterolemic rabbit's artery, showcasing a fatty streak characterized by the presence of intimal macrophage-derived foam cells, which are highlighted by arrows.

caption: Atherosclerotic lesions. (B) Aorta with severe diffuse complicated lesions, including an ulcerated plaque (open arrow), and a lesion with overlying thrombus (closed arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an aorta exhibiting severe diffuse complicated atherosclerotic lesions. Key features include an ulcerated plaque indicated by an open arrow and a lesion with overlying thrombus marked by a closed arrow, highlighting the advanced state of atherosclerosis.

caption: Atherosclerotic lesions. (A) Aorta with mild atherosclerosis composed of fibrous plaques, one denoted by the arrow.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an aorta exhibiting mild atherosclerosis, characterized by the presence of fibrous plaques. One such plaque is specifically indicated by an arrow.

caption: Atherosclerotic plaque, coronary artery. (C) High-power view of the junction of the fibrous cap and core, showing scattered inflammatory cells, calcification (arrowheads), and neovascularization (small arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological cross-section of a coronary artery exhibiting an atherosclerotic plaque. The high-power view highlights the junction between the fibrous cap and the plaque core, revealing scattered inflammatory cells, calcification, and neovascularization.

caption: Atherosclerotic plaque, coronary artery. (A) Overall architecture demonstrating fibrous cap (F) and a central necrotic (largely lipid) core (C); collagen (blue) is stained with Masson trichrome. The lumen (L) is moderately narrowed by this eccentric lesion, which leaves part of the vessel wall unaffected (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a coronary artery exhibiting an atherosclerotic plaque. The Masson trichrome stain highlights the plaque's architecture, revealing a fibrous cap (stained blue) overlying a central necrotic core (stained yellow/green, largely lipid). The plaque is eccentric, causing moderate narrowing of the vessel lumen (L), while a portion of the vessel wall remains unaffected.

caption: Atherosclerotic plaque, coronary artery. (B) Moderate-power view of the plaque shown in A, stained for elastin (black); the internal and external elastic membranes are attenuated, and the media of the artery is thinned under the most advanced plaque (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a coronary artery exhibiting an atherosclerotic plaque. The tissue is stained for elastin, which appears black, highlighting the structural components of the vessel wall. Key pathological features include the attenuation of the internal and external elastic membranes and the thinning of the arterial media beneath the advanced plaque.

caption: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture. (B) Acute coronary throm- bosis superimposed on an atherosclerotic plaque with focal disruption of the fibrous cap, triggering fatal myocardial infarction. In both A and B, an arrow points to the site of plaque rupture. (B, Reproduced from Schoen FJ: Interventional and surgical cardiovascular pathology: clinical correlations and basic principles, Philadelphia, 1989, Saunders, p 61.)
summary: The image depicts an atherosclerotic plaque with focal disruption of the fibrous cap, leading to acute coronary thrombosis. This pathological finding is associated with fatal myocardial infarction.

caption: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture. (A) Plaque rupture without superimposed thrombus, in a patient who died suddenly.
summary: The image displays a histological cross-section of a blood vessel demonstrating an atherosclerotic plaque that has ruptured. The plaque is characterized by a fibrous cap which has been breached, exposing the underlying necrotic core. The caption indicates this is a case of plaque rupture without the formation of a superimposed thrombus, found in a patient who died suddenly.

caption: Cystic medial degeneration. (A) Cross-section of aortic media from a patient with Marfan syndrome, showing marked elastin fragmentation and areas devoid of elastin that resemble cystic spaces (asterisks).
summary: The image displays a histological cross-section of the aortic media from a patient with Marfan syndrome. It demonstrates the pathological feature of cystic medial degeneration, characterized by significant fragmentation of the elastic fibers and the presence of empty, cystic-like spaces where elastin is absent.

caption: Abdominal aortic aneurysm. (B) Opened aorta, with the location of the rupture tract indicated by a probe. The wall of the aneurysm is attenuated, and the lumen is filled by a large, layered thrombus.
summary: The image depicts an abdominal aortic aneurysm, specifically showing the opened aorta with a rupture tract indicated by a probe. The aneurysm wall is attenuated, and the lumen is filled with a large, layered thrombus.

caption: Abdominal aortic aneurysm. (A) External site of rupture of a large aortic aneurysm is indicated by the arrow.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an abdominal aortic aneurysm that has ruptured externally. A metal probe or needle points to the site of rupture on the outer surface of the aneurysm sac, which appears as a large, dilated, and hemorrhagic structure.

caption: Aortic dissection. (B) Histologic preparation showing the dissection and intramural hematoma (asterisk). Aortic elastic layers are black, and blood is red in this section, stained with Movat stain.
summary: The image displays a gross specimen of an aortic dissection, characterized by the separation of the aortic wall layers. A histologic section reveals the dissection plane and an intramural hematoma, with the aortic elastic layers appearing black and the blood appearing red, as visualized by Movat staining.

caption: Aortic dissection. (A) An opened aorta with a proximal dissection originating from a small, oblique intimal tear (identified by the probe) associated with an intramural hematoma. Note that the intimal tear occurred in a region largely free of atherosclerotic plaque. The distal edge of the intramural hema- toma (black arrows) lies at the edge of a large area of atherosclerosis (white arrow), which arrested the propagation of the dissection.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an opened aorta exhibiting an aortic dissection. A proximal dissection is visible, originating from a small, oblique intimal tear (indicated by a probe). An intramural hematoma is present, with its distal edge marked by black arrows. The dissection propagation was arrested at the edge of a large area of atherosclerosis, indicated by a white arrow. Notably, the intimal tear occurred in a region largely free of atherosclerotic plaque.

caption: Temporal (giant cell) arteritis. (B) Elastic tissue stain demonstrating focal destruction of the internal elastic membrane (arrow) and medial attenuation and scarring.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a blood vessel affected by Temporal (giant cell) arteritis. An elastic tissue stain reveals focal destruction of the internal elastic membrane, indicated by an arrow, alongside medial attenuation and scarring.

caption: Temporal (giant cell) arteritis. (A) Hematoxylin-eosin-stained section of a temporal artery showing giant cells near the fragmented internal elastic membrane (arrow), along with medial and adventitial inflammation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a temporal artery affected by temporal (giant cell) arteritis. The Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) stain reveals significant pathological changes, including the presence of giant cells near a fragmented internal elastic membrane and inflammation within the vessel wall.

caption: Polyarteritis nodosa, associated with segmental fibrinoid necrosis and thrombotic occlusion of a small artery. Note that part of the vessel (upper-right, arrow) is uninvolved.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a small artery affected by Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN). The vessel exhibits segmental fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall and thrombotic occlusion. An arrow points to a portion of the vessel wall that remains uninvolved, contrasting with the necrotic segment.

caption: ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. (C) Lung from a patient with granulomatosis with polyangiitis, demonstrating large nodular cavitating lesions. (A, Courtesy of Scott Granter, MD, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, Boston, Massachusetts. C, Courtesy of Sidney Murphree, MD, Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung from a patient with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), an ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. The lung exhibits large nodular cavitating lesions, which are characteristic of GPA.
entities: University of Texas Southwestern Medical School

caption: ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. (B) Vasculitis of a small artery with adjacent granulomatous inflammation including giant cells (arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a small artery exhibiting vasculitis, characterized by inflammation of the vessel wall. Adjacent to the vessel, there is a granulomatous inflammatory infiltrate containing multinucleated giant cells, consistent with ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis.

caption: ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. (A) Microscopic polyangiitis (leukocytoclastic vasculitis) with fragmented neutrophils in the thickened vessel wall. (B and C) Granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue exhibiting ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. It features a thickened vessel wall containing fragmented neutrophils, characteristic of leukocytoclastic vasculitis (Microscopic Polyangiitis), alongside features of Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis.

caption: Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease). The lumen is occluded by thrombus containing a sterile abscess (arrow), and the vessel wall is infiltrated with leukocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a blood vessel affected by Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease). The vessel lumen is occluded by a thrombus containing a sterile abscess, indicated by an arrow, and the vessel wall shows infiltration with leukocytes.

caption: Bacillary angiomatosis. (A) Characteristic cutaneous lesion.
summary: The image displays a characteristic cutaneous lesion identified as Bacillary angiomatosis. The lesion presents as a raised, reddish-brown, lobulated nodule on the skin surface, accompanied by smaller satellite papules.

caption: Bacillary angiomatosis. (B) Histologic features are those of acute inflammation and capillary proliferation. Inset, Modified silver (Warthin-Starry) stain demonstrates clusters of tangled bacilli (black). (A, Courtesy of Richard Johnson, MD, Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Boston, Massachusetts. B and inset, courtesy of Scott Granter, MD, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, Boston, Massachusetts.)
summary: The image displays histologic features of bacillary angiomatosis, characterized by acute inflammation and capillary proliferation. An inset shows a modified silver (Warthin-Starry) stain highlighting clusters of tangled bacilli.

caption: Kaposi sarcoma. (A) Characteristic coalescent cutaneous red-purple macules and plaques.
summary: The image displays a clinical presentation of Kaposi sarcoma, characterized by coalescent cutaneous red-purple macules and plaques on the skin surface.

caption: Kaposi sarcoma. (B) Histologic view of the nodular stage, demonstrating sheets of plump, proliferating spindle cells and slitlike vascular spaces.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Kaposi sarcoma in its nodular stage, characterized by sheets of plump, proliferating spindle cells and slitlike vascular spaces.

caption: Angiosarcoma. (A) Angiosarcoma of the right ventricle.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart, specifically showing a cross-section of the right ventricle. The tissue exhibits a large, irregular, dark brown to black tumor mass with a hemorrhagic and necrotic appearance, consistent with the diagnosis of angiosarcoma.

caption: Angiosarcoma. (B) Moderately differentiated angiosarcoma with dense clumps of atypical cells lining distinct vascular lumina.
summary: The image displays a histological section of moderately differentiated angiosarcoma, characterized by dense clusters of atypical cells forming distinct vascular lumina.

caption: Angiosarcoma. (C) Immunohistochemical staining of angiosarcoma for the endothelial cell marker CD31.
summary: The image displays a histological section of angiosarcoma, a malignant vascular tumor. The tissue exhibits a complex, irregular architecture with slit-like vascular spaces. Immunohistochemical staining for the endothelial cell marker CD31 is positive, indicated by the brown staining of the neoplastic cells, confirming their endothelial origin.

caption: Restenosis after angioplasty and stenting. (A) Gross view demonstrating residual atherosclerotic plaque (arrows) and a new, glistening intimal proliferative lesion.
summary: The image shows a gross pathological specimen of a blood vessel exhibiting restenosis following angioplasty and stenting. The specimen displays a residual atherosclerotic plaque and a new, glistening intimal proliferative lesion, indicating tissue overgrowth within the vessel lumen.

caption: Restenosis after angioplasty and stenting. (B) Histologic view shows a thickened neointima overlying the stent wires (black diamond indicated by the arrow), which encroaches on the lumen (asterisk). (B, Reproduced from Schoen FJ, Edwards WD: Pathology of cardiovascular interventions, including endovascular therapies, revascularization, vascular replacement, cardiac assist/replacement, arrhythmia control, and repaired congenital heart disease. In Silver MD, et al., editors: Cardiovascular pathology, ed 3, Philadelphia, 2001, Churchill Livingstone.)
summary: The image displays a histologic section of a blood vessel demonstrating restenosis following angioplasty and stenting. A thickened neointima is observed overlying the stent wires, which encroach upon the vessel lumen, contributing to the restenosis.

caption: Ventricular septal defect of the membranous type (arrow). (Cour- tesy of William D. Edwards, MD, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota.)
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical specimen of a heart, specifically highlighting a ventricular septal defect of the membranous type, indicated by an arrow.

caption: Coarctation of the aorta with (“infantile” or preductal form) and without a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) (“adult” or postductal form); arrow indicates direction of blood flow. Ao, Aorta; LA, left atrium; LV, left ventricle; PT, pulmonary trunk; RA, right atrium; RV, right ventricle.
summary: The image illustrates two anatomical diagrams of the heart comparing the 'infantile' (preductal) and 'adult' (postductal) forms of coarctation of the aorta. The left diagram shows the preductal form with a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), while the right diagram depicts the postductal form without a PDA. An arrow indicates the direction of blood flow in both configurations, highlighting the altered hemodynamics associated with aortic coarctation.

caption: Coarctation of the aorta, postductal type. The coarctation is a segmental narrowing of the aorta (arrow). Such lesions typically manifest later in life than preductal coarctations. The dilated ascending aorta and major branch vessels are to the left of the coarctation. The lower extremities are perfused predominantly by way of dilated, tortuous collateral channels. (Cour- tesy of Sid Murphree, MD, Department of Pathology, University of Texas South- western Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: This image displays a gross anatomical specimen of the heart and aorta demonstrating a postductal coarctation of the aorta. The pathology is characterized by a distinct segmental narrowing of the aorta (indicated by an arrow) located distal to the ductus arteriosus. Associated with this narrowing is a dilated ascending aorta and major branch vessels to the left of the constriction. To compensate for the obstruction, the lower extremities are perfused via dilated, tortuous collateral channels.

caption: Acute myocardial infarct of the posterolateral left ventricle demonstrated by a lack of triphenyltetrazolium chloride staining in areas of necrosis (arrow); the absence of staining is due to enzyme leakage after cell death. Note the anterior scar (arrowhead), indicative of remote infarction. The myocardial hemorrhage at the right edge of the infarct (asterisk) is due to ventricular rupture, and was the acute cause of death in this patient (specimen is oriented with the posterior wall at the top).
summary: The image shows a cross-section of the heart with an acute myocardial infarct in the posterolateral left ventricle, identified by the lack of triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) staining in necrotic areas. An anterior scar indicates a previous infarction, and myocardial hemorrhage at the right edge of the infarct suggests ventricular rupture, which was the cause of death.

caption: Reperfused myocardial infarction. (A) The transverse heart slice (stained with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride) exhibits a large anterior wall myo- cardial infarction that is hemorrhagic because of bleeding from damaged vessels. The posterior wall is at the top.
summary: The image displays a transverse section of a heart stained with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride, revealing a large anterior wall myocardial infarction characterized by hemorrhage due to bleeding from damaged vessels. The posterior wall is located at the top of the slice.

caption: Reperfused myocardial infarction. (B) Hemorrhage and contraction bands, visible as prominent hypereosinophilic cross-striations spanning myofibers (arrow), are seen microscopically.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of reperfused myocardial infarction, characterized by hemorrhage and prominent hypereosinophilic cross-striations (contraction bands) spanning the myofibers.

caption: Hypertensive heart disease. (A) Systemic (left-sided) hypertensive heart disease. There is marked concentric thickening of the left ventricular wall causing reduction in lumen size. The left ventricle and left atrium are shown on the right in this four-chamber view of the heart. A pacemaker is present incidentally in the right ventricle (arrow). Note also the left atrial dilation (asterisk) due to stiffening of the left ventricle and impaired diastolic relaxation, leading to atrial volume overload.
summary: The image displays a four-chamber view of a heart affected by systemic (left-sided) hypertensive heart disease, characterized by marked concentric thickening of the left ventricular wall, which reduces the lumen size. Additionally, there is left atrial dilation due to impaired diastolic relaxation and volume overload. An incidental pacemaker is visible in the right ventricle.

caption: Hypertensive heart disease. (B) Chronic cor pulmonale. The right ventricle (shown on the left) is markedly dilated and hypertrophied with a thickened free wall and hypertrophied trabeculae. The shape and volume of the left ventricle have been distorted by the enlarged right ventricle.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting chronic cor pulmonale, characterized by marked dilation and hypertrophy of the right ventricle with thickened free wall and hypertrophied trabeculae. The enlarged right ventricle has distorted the shape and volume of the left ventricle, consistent with hypertensive heart disease.

caption: Myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve.
