There is promi- nent hooding with prolapse of the posterior mitral leaflet (arrow) into the left atrium; the atrium also is dilated, reflecting long-standing valvular insuf- ficiency and volume overload. The left ventricle is shown on the right in this four-chamber view.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart, specifically a four-chamber view, demonstrating myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve. Key features include prominent hooding and prolapse of the posterior mitral leaflet into the dilated left atrium, indicative of long-standing valvular insufficiency and volume overload. The left ventricle is also visible.

caption: Major forms of vegetative endocarditis. The acute rheumatic fever phase of rheumatic heart disease (RHD) is marked by the appearance of small, warty, inflammatory vegetations along the lines of valve closure; as the inflammation resolves, substantial scarring can result. Infective endocarditis (IE) is characterized by large, irregular, often destructive masses that can extend from valve leaflets onto adjacent structures (e.g., chordae or myocardium). Non- bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE) typically manifests with small- to medium-sized, bland, nondestructive vegetations at the line of valve closure. Libman-Sacks endocarditis (LSE) is characterized by small- to medium-sized inflammatory vegetations that can be attached on either side of the valve leaflets; these heal with scarring.
summary: The image illustrates four major forms of vegetative endocarditis: acute rheumatic fever phase of rheumatic heart disease (RHD), infective endocarditis (IE), non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE), and Libman-Sacks endocarditis (LSE). Each form is depicted with distinct vegetations on heart valves, showing differences in size, location, and pathological characteristics.
entities: attached on either side of valve leaflets

caption: Infective endocarditis. (A) Subacute endocarditis caused by Streptococcus viridans on a previously myxomatous mitral valve. The large, friable vegeta- tions are denoted by arrows.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart with subacute infective endocarditis caused by Streptococcus viridans on a previously myxomatous mitral valve. Large, friable vegetations are visible on the valve surface, indicated by arrows.

caption: Infective endocarditis. (B) Acute endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus on a congenitally bicuspid aortic valve with extensive cuspal destruction and ring abscess (arrow).
summary: The image depicts a gross pathology specimen of the heart showing acute infective endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus. The pathology is localized to a congenitally bicuspid aortic valve, which exhibits extensive destruction of the valve cusps and a large abscess at the valve ring (indicated by an arrow).

caption: The three major forms of cardiomyopathy. Dilated cardiomyopa- thy leads primarily to systolic dysfunction, whereas restrictive and hypertro- phic cardiomyopathies result in diastolic dysfunction. Note the changes in atrial and/or ventricular dilation and in ventricular wall thickness. Ao, Aorta; LA, left atrium; LV, left ventricle.
summary: The image displays a schematic diagram illustrating the three major forms of cardiomyopathy: dilated, restrictive, and hypertrophic. It highlights the structural differences in the heart chambers, specifically the left ventricle (LV) and left atrium (LA), and the aorta (Ao). The diagram contrasts how dilated cardiomyopathy causes systolic dysfunction with enlarged chambers, while restrictive and hypertrophic cardiomyopathies lead to diastolic dysfunction, characterized by thickened ventricular walls and varying degrees of chamber dilation.

caption: The three major forms of cardiomyopathy. Dilated cardiomyopa- thy leads primarily to systolic dysfunction, whereas restrictive and hypertro- phic cardiomyopathies result in diastolic dysfunction. Note the changes in atrial and/or ventricular dilation and in ventricular wall thickness. Ao, Aorta; LA, left atrium; LV, left ventricle.
summary: The image displays a schematic diagram illustrating the three major forms of cardiomyopathy: dilated, restrictive, and hypertrophic. It highlights the structural differences in the heart chambers, specifically the left atrium (LA) and left ventricle (LV), and the aorta (Ao). The diagram contrasts how dilated cardiomyopathy causes systolic dysfunction with enlarged chambers, while restrictive and hypertrophic cardiomyopathies lead to diastolic dysfunction, characterized by thickened ventricular walls and varying degrees of chamber dilation.

caption: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). (A) Four-chamber dilation and hypertrophy are evident. A small mural thrombus can be seen at the apex of the left ventricle (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting features of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). The specimen shows significant dilation of the heart chambers, particularly the ventricles, accompanied by hypertrophy of the ventricular walls. A mural thrombus is visible at the apex of the left ventricle.

caption: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). (B) The nonspecific histologic picture in typical DCM, with myocyte hypertrophy and interstitial fibrosis. (Collagen is blue in this Masson trichrome–stained preparation.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of heart tissue exhibiting features of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). The Masson trichrome stain highlights myocyte hypertrophy (enlarged muscle cells appearing red/pink) and significant interstitial fibrosis (connective tissue appearing blue) within the myocardium.

caption: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy. (B) The right ventricular myocardium (red) is focally replaced by fibrous connective tissue (blue, arrow) and fat (Masson trichrome stain).
summary: The image displays a histological section of the right ventricular myocardium stained with Masson trichrome. It illustrates the pathological features of Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC), characterized by the focal replacement of the red-stained myocardial tissue with blue-stained fibrous connective tissue and white-stained adipose (fat) tissue.

caption: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy. (A) The right ventricle is markedly dilated with focal, almost transmural replacement of the free wall by adipose tissue and fibrosis. The left ventricle has a grossly normal appearance in this heart; it can be involved (albeit to a lesser extent) in some instances.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC). The right ventricle is significantly dilated, and its free wall shows extensive replacement by yellow adipose tissue and fibrous tissue, indicating the characteristic fatty infiltration of the disease. The left ventricle appears relatively normal in this specific cross-section.

caption: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy. (A) The septal muscle bulges into the left ventricular outflow tract, giving rise to a “banana-shaped” ventricular lumen, and the left atrium is enlarged. The anterior mitral leaflet has been moved away from the septum to reveal a fibrous endocardial plaque (arrow) (see text).
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical specimen of a heart exhibiting hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy. The septal muscle is visibly bulging into the left ventricular outflow tract, creating a 'banana-shaped' ventricular lumen, and the left atrium is enlarged. An anterior mitral leaflet has been retracted to reveal a fibrous endocardial plaque indicated by an arrow.

caption: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy. (B) Histologic appearance demonstrating disarray, extreme hypertrophy, and characteristic branching of myocytes, as well as interstitial fibrosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of cardiac tissue exhibiting features of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, specifically characterized by disarray, extreme hypertrophy, branching of myocytes, and interstitial fibrosis.

caption: Acute suppurative (purulent, exudative) pericarditis, caused by extension from a pneumonia.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting acute suppurative (purulent, exudative) pericarditis, characterized by the presence of purulent exudate on the pericardial surface. This condition is caused by the extension of infection from an adjacent pneumonia.

caption: Atrial myxoma. (A) A large pedunculated lesion arises from the region of the fossa ovalis and extends into the mitral valve orifice.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an atrial myxoma, characterized by a loose, myxoid stroma containing scattered spindle cells and stellate cells. The caption describes the lesion as a large pedunculated mass arising from the region of the fossa ovalis and extending into the mitral valve orifice.

caption: Atrial myxoma. (B) Abun- dant amorphous extracellular matrix contains scattered multinucleate myxoma cells (arrowheads) in various groupings, including abnormal vascular formations (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart, specifically highlighting an atrial myxoma. The caption describes the microscopic features of the tumor, noting an abundant amorphous extracellular matrix containing scattered multinucleate myxoma cells and abnormal vascular formations.

caption: Carcinoid heart disease. (A) Characteristic endocardial fibrotic (light gray) lesion “coating” the right ventricle and tricuspid valve, and extending onto the chordae tendineae.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting carcinoid heart disease. The right ventricle and tricuspid valve are covered by a distinct, light gray, fibrotic lesion that appears to coat the endocardial surface and extends onto the chordae tendineae.

caption: Carcinoid heart disease. (B) Microscopic appearance of the thickened endocardium, which contains smooth muscle cells and abundant acid mucopolysac- charides (blue-green in this Movat stain, which colors the underlying endocardial elastic tissue black).
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of the heart's endocardium affected by carcinoid heart disease. The endocardium is notably thickened and contains smooth muscle cells and abundant acid mucopolysaccharides, which appear blue-green due to the Movat stain. The underlying endocardial elastic tissue is stained black.

caption: Hereditary spherocytosis—peripheral blood smear. Note the anisocytosis and several hyperchromic spherocytes. Howell-Jolly bodies (small nuclear remnants) are also present in the red cells of this asplenic patient.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with hereditary spherocytosis, an asplenic condition. Key features include anisocytosis (variation in red blood cell size), hyperchromic spherocytes (small, dense, round red blood cells), and the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies (small nuclear remnants) within red blood cells.

caption: Sickle cell anemia—peripheral blood smear. (A) Low magnification shows sickle cells, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, and target cells.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with sickle cell anemia, revealing characteristic morphological abnormalities including sickle-shaped red blood cells (sickle cells), variation in cell size (anisocytosis), variation in cell shape (poikilocytosis), and target cells.

caption: Sickle cell anemia—peripheral blood smear. (B) Higher mag- nification shows an irreversibly sickled cell in the center.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with sickle cell anemia, highlighting the characteristic sickle-shaped red blood cells. A higher magnification view reveals an irreversibly sickled cell in the center, demonstrating the morphological changes associated with this genetic disorder.

caption: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency after oxidant drug exposure—peripheral blood smear. (Inset) Red cells with precipitates of denatured globin (Heinz bodies) shown by supravital staining. As the splenic macrophages pluck out these inclusions, “bite cells” similar to the one in this smear are produced.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency following exposure to an oxidant drug. The main field shows numerous red blood cells, some of which are 'bite cells'—cells that have had a portion of their membrane removed. An inset shows red cells with Heinz bodies, which are precipitates of denatured globin, visualized using supravital staining.

caption: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia—peripheral blood smear. This specimen from a patient with hemolytic uremic syndrome contains several fragmented red cells.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with hemolytic uremic syndrome, characterized by the presence of fragmented red blood cells (schistocytes) alongside normal appearing red blood cells.

caption: Iron deficiency anemia—peripheral blood smear. Note the increased central pallor of most of the red cells. Scattered, fully hemoglo- binized cells, from a recent blood transfusion, stand out in contrast.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear indicative of iron deficiency anemia. The majority of red blood cells exhibit increased central pallor, a characteristic feature of this condition. Additionally, there are scattered, fully hemoglobinized red blood cells present, which are identified as being from a recent blood transfusion.

caption: Megaloblastic anemia. A peripheral blood smear shows a hyper- segmented neutrophil with a six-lobed nucleus.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear characteristic of megaloblastic anemia, featuring a hyper-segmented neutrophil with a six-lobed nucleus and surrounding red blood cells.

caption: Atypical lymphocytes in infectious mononucleosis.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a blood smear showing atypical lymphocytes, which are characteristic of infectious mononucleosis. The atypical lymphocytes are larger than normal lymphocytes and have irregular shapes, set against a background of normal red blood cells.

caption: Follicular lymphoma—lymph node. (A) Nodular aggregates of lymphoma cells are present throughout.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node exhibiting follicular lymphoma. The visual evidence shows a nodular architecture composed of aggregates of lymphoma cells, which are the neoplastic cells characteristic of this condition.

caption: Follicular lymphoma—lymph node. (B) At high magnification, small lymphoid cells with condensed chromatin and irregular or cleaved nuclear outlines (centrocytes) are mixed with a population of larger cells with nucleoli (centroblasts). (A, Courtesy of Dr. Robert W. McKenna, Department of Pathology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a high-magnification view of a lymph node biopsy stained with H&E, showing a mixed population of small lymphoid cells with condensed chromatin and irregular or cleaved nuclear outlines (centrocytes) intermixed with larger cells possessing prominent nucleoli (centroblasts), which is characteristic of follicular lymphoma.

caption: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma—lymph node. The tumor cells have large nuclei with open chromatin and prominent nucleoli.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node exhibiting diffuse large B cell lymphoma. The tissue is characterized by a diffuse infiltration of large neoplastic cells. These tumor cells possess large nuclei with open chromatin patterns and prominent nucleoli, which are key diagnostic features of this lymphoma subtype.

caption: Burkitt lymphoma—lymph node. The tumor cells and their nuclei are fairly uniform, giving a monotonous appearance. Note the high level of mitotic activity (arrowheads) and prominent nucleoli. The “starry sky” pattern produced by interspersed, lightly staining, normal macrophages is better appreciated at a lower magnification.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node affected by Burkitt lymphoma. The tumor cells exhibit a monotonous appearance with uniform nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and a high level of mitotic activity. The characteristic 'starry sky' pattern is visible, formed by interspersed, lightly staining normal macrophages.

caption: Hodgkin lymphoma—lymph node. A binucleate Reed-Sternberg cell with large, inclusion-like nucleoli and abundant cytoplasm is surrounded by lymphocytes, macrophages, and an eosinophil.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node affected by Hodgkin lymphoma. The central feature is a binucleate Reed-Sternberg cell, characterized by large, inclusion-like nucleoli and abundant cytoplasm. This cell is surrounded by a mixed inflammatory infiltrate consisting of lymphocytes, macrophages, and an eosinophil.

caption: Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular sclerosis type—lymph node. A low- power view shows well-defined bands of pink, acellular collagen that have subdivided the tumor cells into nodules.
summary: The image displays a low-power microscopic view of a lymph node affected by Hodgkin lymphoma, specifically the nodular sclerosis subtype. The visual field is characterized by a dense population of tumor cells that are compartmentalized into distinct nodules by thick, eosinophilic bands of collagen.

caption: Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular sclerosis type—lymph node. A low- power view shows well-defined bands of pink, acellular collagen that have subdivided the tumor cells into nodules.
summary: The image displays a low-power histological view of a lymph node affected by Hodgkin lymphoma, specifically the nodular sclerosis subtype. The visual evidence highlights the characteristic architecture where bands of dense, eosinophilic (pink) collagenous tissue divide the neoplastic cells into distinct nodules.

caption: Hodgkin lymphoma, mixed-cellularity type—lymph node. A diag- nostic, binucleate Reed-Sternberg cell is surrounded by eosinophils, lympho- cytes, and histiocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node exhibiting Hodgkin lymphoma, specifically the mixed-cellularity subtype. The field is characterized by a mixed inflammatory infiltrate containing lymphocytes, eosinophils, and histiocytes, within which a diagnostic binucleate Reed-Sternberg cell is identified.

caption: Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular lymphocyte-predominant type— lymph node. Numerous mature-looking lymphocytes surround scattered, large, pale-staining lymphocytic and histiocytic variants (“popcorn” cells).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node exhibiting Hodgkin lymphoma, specifically the nodular lymphocyte-predominant type. The field is densely populated with numerous mature-looking lymphocytes, interspersed with scattered large, pale-staining cells identified as lymphocytic and histiocytic variants, also known as 'popcorn' cells.

caption: Acute promyelocytic leukemia—bone marrow aspirate. The neo- plastic promyelocytes have abnormally coarse and numerous azurophilic granules. Other characteristic findings include a cell in the center of the field with multiple needlelike Auer rods.
summary: The image displays a bone marrow aspirate from a patient with Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL). It features a cluster of neoplastic promyelocytes characterized by abnormally coarse and numerous azurophilic granules. A distinct cell in the center of the field exhibits multiple needle-like Auer rods, a hallmark of this condition. The background contains numerous red blood cells.

caption: Acute promyelocytic leukemia—bone marrow aspirate. The neo- plastic promyelocytes have abnormally coarse and numerous azurophilic granules. Other characteristic findings include a cell in the center of the field with multiple needlelike Auer rods.
summary: The image displays a bone marrow aspirate from a patient with Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL). It features clusters of neoplastic promyelocytes characterized by abundant, coarse, and numerous azurophilic granules. A key diagnostic feature visible is a cell containing multiple needle-like Auer rods, which are pathognomonic for this condition.

caption: CML—peripheral blood smear. Granulocytic forms at various stages of differentiation are present.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). It shows a significant increase in granulocytic cells, including myelocytes, metamyelocytes, and bands, indicating a proliferation of granulocytes at various stages of maturation.

caption: Primary myelofibrosis—peripheral blood smear. Two nucleated erythroid precursors and several teardrop-shaped red cells (dacryocytes) are evident. Immature myeloid cells were present in other fields. An identical histologic picture can be seen in other diseases producing marrow distortion and fibrosis.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with Primary Myelofibrosis. Key features include the presence of nucleated erythroid precursors and teardrop-shaped red blood cells (dacryocytes). The caption notes that immature myeloid cells were also observed in other fields of the smear. This histologic pattern is characteristic of marrow distortion and fibrosis, which can also be seen in other related diseases.

caption: The normal alveolus (left) and the injured alveolus in the early phase of acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome. Under the influence of proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukins IL-8 and IL-1 and tumor necrosis factor (TNF) (released by macrophages), neutrophils are sequestered in the pulmonary microvasculature and then egress into the alveolar space, where they undergo activation. Activated neutrophils release leukot- rienes, oxidants, proteases, and platelet-activating factor (PAF), which contribute to local tissue damage, accumulation of edema fluid, surfactant inactivation, and hyaline membrane formation. Subsequently, the release of macrophage-derived fibrogenic cytokines such as transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) and platelet-derived growth factor (PGDF) stimulate fibroblast growth and collagen deposition associated with the healing phase of injury. (Modified from Ware LB: Pathophysiology of acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome, Semin Respir Crit Care Med 27:337, 2006.)
summary: The image illustrates the transition from a normal alveolus to an injured alveolus during the early phase of acute lung injury and acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). It depicts the sequestration and activation of neutrophils in the pulmonary microvasculature, their migration into the alveolar space, and the subsequent release of inflammatory mediators that cause tissue damage, edema, surfactant inactivation, and hyaline membrane formation. The caption also references the later healing phase involving fibroblast growth and collagen deposition stimulated by cytokines such as TGF-β and PDGF.

caption: Pathogenesis of emphysema. See text for details.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of emphysema, showing a progression from normal alveolar structure to severe destruction. It depicts four stages: (1) normal lung tissue with intact alveoli, (2) early emphysema with some alveolar wall destruction, (3) moderate emphysema with more extensive alveolar wall destruction, and (4) severe emphysema with significant alveolar destruction and enlarged air spaces.

caption: Pulmonary emphysema. There is marked enlargement of the air spaces, with destruction of alveolar septa but without fibrosis. Note the presence of black anthracotic pigment.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting pulmonary emphysema. Key features include the marked enlargement of air spaces and the destruction of alveolar septa, occurring without fibrosis. Additionally, black anthracotic pigment is visible within the tissue.

caption: Bullous emphysema with large apical and subpleural bullae. (From the Teaching Collection of the Department of Pathology, University of Texas South- western Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of lungs exhibiting bullous emphysema, characterized by the presence of large, air-filled bullae located in the apical and subpleural regions. The lung tissue appears distorted and expanded due to these cystic spaces.
entities: University of Texas Southwestern Medical School

caption: Chronic bronchitis. The lumen of the bronchus is above. Note the marked thickening of the mucous gland layer (approximately twice-normal) and squamous metaplasia of lung epithelium. (From the Teaching Collection of the Department of Pathology, University of Texas, Southwestern Medical School, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of chronic bronchitis, characterized by significant thickening of the mucous gland layer within the bronchial lumen and squamous metaplasia of the lung epithelium.

caption: Bronchial biopsy specimen from an asthmatic patient showing sub–basement membrane fibrosis, eosinophilic inflammation, and smooth muscle hyperplasia.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a bronchial biopsy from a patient with asthma. The tissue exhibits characteristic pathological features including sub-basement membrane fibrosis, eosinophilic inflammation, and smooth muscle hyperplasia.

caption: Bronchiectasis in a patient with cystic fibrosis who underwent lung resection for transplantation. Cut surface of lung shows markedly dilated bronchi filled with purulent mucus that extend to subpleural regions.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung resected from a patient with cystic fibrosis. The cut surface reveals markedly dilated bronchi filled with purulent mucus, extending to the subpleural regions, characteristic of bronchiectasis.

caption: Usual interstitial pneumonia. The fibrosis, which varies in inten- sity, is more pronounced in the subpleural region.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP). The caption highlights that the fibrosis is more pronounced in the subpleural region, which is a characteristic feature of UIP. The tissue shows dense fibrosis and architectural distortion.

caption: Usual interstitial pneumonia. Fibroblastic focus with fibers running parallel to surface and bluish myxoid extracellular matrix. Honey- combing is present to the left.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting features of Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP). Key pathological findings include a fibroblastic focus characterized by proliferating fibroblasts and myofibroblasts within a loose, bluish myxoid extracellular matrix. The adjacent alveolar spaces show honeycombing, which is a hallmark of advanced fibrosis in UIP, consisting of cystic airspaces lined by bronchiolar epithelium.

caption: Advanced silicosis, seen in a transected lung. Scarring has con- tracted the upper lobe into a small dark mass (arrow). Note the dense pleural thickening.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lung exhibiting advanced silicosis. The upper lobe is contracted into a small, dark mass due to extensive scarring, indicated by an arrow. Additionally, there is notable dense pleural thickening visible in the tissue.

caption: Advanced silicosis, seen in a transected lung. Scarring has con- tracted the upper lobe into a small dark mass (arrow). Note the dense pleural thickening.
summary: The image displays a transected lung specimen exhibiting advanced silicosis. Key pathological features include a contracted upper lobe forming a small, dark mass due to scarring, and significant pleural thickening.

caption: Coalescent collagenous silicotic nodules.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing coalescent collagenous silicotic nodules. The nodules appear as large, pink, fibrous masses composed of collagen fibers, which have merged together. These nodules are surrounded by lung parenchyma containing alveolar spaces and adipose tissue.

caption: High-power detail of an asbestos body, revealing the typical beading and knobbed ends (arrow).
summary: The image displays a high-power microscopic view of a tissue section stained with H&E, highlighting a specific pathological finding known as an asbestos body. The structure is characterized by its distinct morphology, featuring a central core with beading and knobbed ends, which are typical features of asbestos fibers that have undergone a reaction with iron in the lung tissue.

caption: Asbestosis. Markedly thickened visceral pleura covers the lateral and diaphragmatic surface of the lung. Note also severe interstitial fibrosis diffusely affecting the lower lobe of the lung.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung affected by asbestosis, characterized by markedly thickened visceral pleura covering the lateral and diaphragmatic surfaces and severe diffuse interstitial fibrosis primarily in the lower lobe.

caption: Sarcoid. Characteristic peribronchial noncaseating granulomas with many giant cells are present.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue, identified as Sarcoidosis. It features characteristic peribronchial noncaseating granulomas containing numerous giant cells, which are key diagnostic features of the condition.

caption: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, histologic appearance. Loosely formed interstitial granulomas and chronic inflammation are characteristic.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting features of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. The key pathological finding is a loosely formed interstitial granuloma surrounded by chronic inflammation, which is characteristic of this condition.

caption: Desquamative interstitial pneumonia. There is accumulation of large numbers of macrophages within the alveolar spaces with only slight fibrous thickening of the alveolar walls.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia (DIP). The primary pathological feature is the accumulation of numerous macrophages within the alveolar spaces. Additionally, there is slight fibrous thickening of the alveolar walls, although the alveolar architecture is largely preserved.

caption: Large saddle embolus from the femoral vein lying astride the main left and right pulmonary arteries.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the heart, specifically the pulmonary trunk and its bifurcation. A large, dark, saddle-shaped thrombus is lodged across the bifurcation, obstructing both the left and right pulmonary arteries. The surrounding tissue includes the heart muscle and other visible anatomical structures.

caption: A small, roughly wedge-shaped hemorrhagic pulmonary infarct of recent occurrence.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung section featuring a small, roughly wedge-shaped hemorrhagic pulmonary infarct, indicative of a recent occurrence.

caption: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage syndrome. (A) Lung biopsy specimen demonstrates large numbers of intraalveolar hemosiderin-laden macrophages on a background of thickened fibrous septa.
summary: The image displays a lung biopsy specimen showing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage syndrome, characterized by numerous intraalveolar hemosiderin-laden macrophages and thickened fibrous septa.

caption: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage syndrome. (B) The tissue has been stained with Prussian blue, an iron stain that highlights the abundant intracellular hemo- siderin. (From the Teaching Collection of the Department of Pathology, Children’s Medical Center, Dallas, Texas.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, characterized by the presence of abundant intracellular hemosiderin deposits highlighted by Prussian blue staining.

caption: Lobar pneumonia with gray hepatization. The lower lobe is uniformly consolidated.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung lobe exhibiting lobar pneumonia with gray hepatization. The lower lobe is uniformly consolidated, appearing firm and solid, contrasting with the upper lobe which shows normal lung parenchyma.

caption: Viral pneumonia. The thickened alveolar walls are infiltrated with lymphocytes and some plasma cells, which are spilling over into alveolar spaces. Note the focal alveolar edema (center) and early fibrosis (upper right).
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue affected by viral pneumonia. The alveolar walls are notably thickened and infiltrated by inflammatory cells, specifically lymphocytes and plasma cells, which are also spilling over into the alveolar spaces. The image highlights focal alveolar edema in the center and early fibrosis in the upper right area.

caption: Primary pulmonary tuberculosis, Ghon complex. The gray-white parenchymal focus (arrow) is under the pleura in the lower part of the upper lobe. Hilar lymph nodes with caseation are seen (left).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung affected by primary pulmonary tuberculosis. It features a gray-white parenchymal focus, identified as a Ghon complex, located under the pleura in the lower part of the upper lobe. Additionally, hilar lymph nodes exhibiting caseation are visible.

caption: Secondary pulmonary tuberculosis. The upper parts of both lungs are riddled with gray-white areas of caseation and multiple areas of softening and cavitation.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of lungs affected by secondary pulmonary tuberculosis. The upper lobes of both lungs exhibit extensive pathology characterized by gray-white areas of caseation, softening, and multiple cavitations.

caption: Miliary tuberculosis of the spleen. The cut surface shows numer- ous gray-white granulomas.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a spleen affected by miliary tuberculosis. The cut surface reveals numerous small, gray-white granulomas scattered throughout the tissue, characteristic of the disseminated nature of this infection.

caption: Cytomegalovirus infection of the lung. A distinct nuclear inclu- sion and multiple cytoplasmic inclusions are seen in an enlarged cell.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue affected by Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection. The key feature is a significantly enlarged cell (cytomegaly) containing a prominent, large intranuclear inclusion body (often described as an 'owl's eye' inclusion) and multiple smaller cytoplasmic inclusions. The surrounding tissue shows alveolar spaces and red blood cells.

caption: Pneumocystis pneumonia. (A) The alveoli are filled with a characteristic foamy acellular exudate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue affected by Pneumocystis pneumonia. The alveolar spaces are filled with a characteristic foamy, acellular exudate, which is a hallmark of this condition.

caption: Pneumocystis pneumonia. (B) Silver stain demonstrates cup-shaped and round cysts within the exudate.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of tissue, specifically an exudate, stained with a silver stain. The stain reveals numerous cup-shaped and round cysts, which are characteristic of Pneumocystis pneumonia.

caption: Squamous cell carcinoma. (A) Squamous cell carcinoma appearing as a central (hilar) mass that is invading contiguous parenchyma.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung exhibiting a central (hilar) mass consistent with squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor appears to be invading the surrounding lung parenchyma, which shows signs of consolidation and discoloration.

caption: Squamous cell carcinoma. (B) Well- differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, showing keratinization and pearls.
summary: The image displays a histological section of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by the presence of keratinization and keratin pearls.

caption: Small cell carcinoma with small deeply basophilic cells and areas of necrosis (top left). Note basophilic staining of vascular walls due to encrus- tation by DNA from necrotic tumor cells (Azzopardi effect).
summary: The image displays a histological section of small cell carcinoma, characterized by small, deeply basophilic tumor cells and areas of necrosis. A key feature highlighted is the basophilic staining of vascular walls, caused by the deposition of DNA from necrotic tumor cells, known as the Azzopardi effect.

caption: Bronchial carcinoid. (A) Carcinoid growing as a spherical, pale mass (arrow) protruding into the lumen of the bronchus.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a bronchial carcinoid tumor. It shows a spherical, pale mass protruding into the lumen of the bronchus, surrounded by red, vascular tissue.

caption: Bronchial carcinoid. (B) Histologic appearance demonstrating small, rounded, uniform nuclei and moderate cytoplasm.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a bronchial carcinoid tumor, characterized by a trabecular or nested growth pattern of uniform, small, rounded cells with moderate cytoplasm.

caption: Malignant mesothelioma. Note the thick, firm, white pleural tumor that ensheathes this bisected lung.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a bisected lung exhibiting malignant mesothelioma. The tumor is characterized as a thick, firm, white mass that completely ensheathes the lung tissue.

caption: Laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma (arrow) arising in a supraglottic location (above the true vocal cord).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a larynx, specifically showing a supraglottic squamous cell carcinoma. A ruler is placed across the specimen for scale, highlighting the tumor's location above the true vocal cords.

caption: Low-power electron micrograph of rat glomerulus. B, Basement membrane; CL, capillary lumen; End, endothelium; Ep, visceral epithelial cells (podocytes) with foot processes; Mes, mesangium; US, urinary space.
summary: This is a low-power transmission electron micrograph of a rat glomerulus, illustrating the ultrastructural components of the renal filtration barrier. Key labeled structures include the capillary lumen, endothelial cells, visceral epithelial cells (podocytes) with their foot processes, the glomerular basement membrane, and the urinary space.

caption: Two patterns of deposition of immune complexes as seen by immunofluorescence microscopy. (A) Granular, characteristic of circulating and in situ immune complex deposition.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a glomerulus stained via immunofluorescence, revealing a granular pattern of green fluorescence. This pattern indicates the deposition of immune complexes, which is characteristic of conditions involving circulating or in situ immune complex deposition.

caption: Two patterns of deposition of immune complexes as seen by immunofluorescence microscopy. (B) Linear, characteristic of classic anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody glomerulonephritis. (A, Courtesy of Dr. J. Kowalewska, Department of Pathology, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington.)
summary: The image displays a glomerulus with linear immunofluorescence staining along the glomerular basement membrane, characteristic of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody glomerulonephritis.

caption: Focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis (periodic acid–Schiff stain), seen as a collection of scarred, obliterated capillaries and accumula- tions of matrix material in part of the affected glomerulus.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney glomerulus stained with Periodic Acid-Schiff (PAS). It illustrates the pathology of Focal and Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), characterized by a distinct area of scarring and sclerosis within a portion of the glomerulus, while the rest of the glomerular structure remains relatively intact.

caption: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis. (A) Glomerular hypercellularity is caused by intracapillary leukocytes and proliferation of intrinsic glomerular cells. Note the red blood cell casts in the tubules.
summary: The image depicts a histological section of kidney tissue showing poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis, characterized by glomerular hypercellularity due to intracapillary leukocytes and proliferation of intrinsic glomerular cells, along with red blood cell casts in the tubules.

caption: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis. (B) Typical electron-dense subepithelial “hump” (arrow) and intramembranous deposits. BM, Basement membrane; CL, capillary lumen; E, endothelial cell; Ep, visceral epithelial cells (podocytes).
summary: The image is an electron micrograph of a kidney glomerulus showing poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis, characterized by a typical electron-dense subepithelial 'hump' (indicated by an arrow) and intramembranous deposits. The labeled structures include the basement membrane (BM), capillary lumen (CL), endothelial cell (E), and visceral epithelial cells (podocytes, Ep).

caption: IgA nephropathy. Characteristic immunofluorescence deposition of IgA, principally in mesangial regions, is evident. IgA, Immunoglobulin A.
summary: The image displays a glomerulus from a kidney biopsy exhibiting characteristic immunofluorescence staining. Bright green fluorescence indicates the deposition of Immunoglobulin A (IgA) primarily within the mesangial regions, which is the hallmark finding of IgA nephropathy.

caption: Crescentic glomerulonephritis (silver stain). Arrows indicate areas of necrosis and crescent formation. The segmental distribution in this case is typical of ANCA (anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody)–associated crescentic glomerulonephritis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney glomerulus stained with silver, demonstrating crescentic glomerulonephritis. Key features include areas of necrosis and the formation of crescents, which are characteristic of ANCA-associated crescentic glomerulonephritis.

caption: Acute pyelonephritis. The cortical surface is studded with focal pale abscesses, more numerous in the upper pole and middle region of the kidney; the lower pole is relatively unaffected. Between the abscesses, there is dark congestion of the renal surface.
summary: The image depicts a gross pathology specimen of a kidney affected by acute pyelonephritis. The cortical surface shows multiple focal pale abscesses, predominantly in the upper pole and middle region, with the lower pole being relatively spared. The areas between the abscesses exhibit dark congestion of the renal surface.

caption: Drug-induced interstitial nephritis, with prominent eosinophilic and mononuclear infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of kidney tissue exhibiting drug-induced interstitial nephritis. The tissue shows a significant inflammatory response characterized by a dense infiltrate of cells, specifically highlighting the presence of eosinophils and mononuclear cells within the interstitial space.

caption: Acute tubular epithelial cell injury with blebbing at the luminal pole, detachment of tubular cells from their underlying basement mem- branes, and granular casts.
summary: The image displays a histological section of kidney tissue, specifically highlighting the renal tubules. The caption describes the presence of acute tubular epithelial cell injury, characterized by cellular blebbing at the luminal pole, detachment of tubular cells from the basement membranes, and the formation of granular casts within the tubules.

caption: Benign nephrosclerosis. High-power view of two arterioles with hyaline deposition, marked thickening of the walls, and a narrowed lumen.
summary: The image displays a high-power histological view of kidney tissue exhibiting benign nephrosclerosis. Key features include two arterioles showing significant hyaline deposition within the vessel walls, resulting in marked thickening and a consequent narrowing of the lumens.

caption: Malignant hypertension. (A) Fibrinoid necrosis of afferent arteriole (periodic acid–Schiff stain).
summary: The image shows a histological section of kidney tissue stained with periodic acid–Schiff (PAS), demonstrating fibrinoid necrosis of the afferent arteriole, a characteristic finding in malignant hypertension.

caption: Malignant hypertension. (B) Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis (onion-skin lesion).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney exhibiting features of malignant hypertension. A glomerulus and surrounding cellular infiltrates are visible. Centrally located, a distinct arteriole demonstrates severe concentric thickening of the walls, creating an 'onion-skin' appearance, which is characteristic of hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis.

caption: Chronic glomerulonephritis. A Masson trichrome preparation shows complete replacement of virtually all glomeruli by blue-staining col- lagen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of kidney tissue, specifically showing chronic glomerulonephritis. The Masson trichrome stain highlights extensive fibrosis, where collagen has replaced the glomerular structures, appearing as blue-staining areas.

caption: Autosomal dominant adult polycystic kidney, viewed from the external surface (A) and bisected
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an adult kidney affected by Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD). The organ is shown from the external surface, revealing a massively enlarged, distorted, and lobulated structure. The surface is studded with numerous cysts of varying sizes, ranging from small indentations to large, fluid-filled sacs that have replaced the normal renal parenchyma. A portion of the renal pelvis or ureter is also visible.

caption: Autosomal dominant adult polycystic kidney, viewed from the external surface (B). The kidney is markedly enlarged (centimeter rule is shown for scale), with numerous dilated cysts.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an adult kidney affected by Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD). The kidney is shown from the external surface, appearing markedly enlarged and distorted by the presence of numerous dilated cysts of varying sizes.

caption: Hydronephrosis of the kidney, with marked dilation of the pelvis and calyces and thinning of renal parenchyma.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting severe hydronephrosis, characterized by the marked dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces, and significant thinning of the renal parenchyma.

caption: Renal cell carcinoma: Representative cross-section showing yel- lowish, spherical neoplasm in one pole of the kidney. Note the tumor in the dilated, thrombosed renal vein.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney affected by renal cell carcinoma. A distinct, yellowish, spherical neoplasm is visible at one pole of the kidney. Additionally, the image highlights a dilated renal vein containing a thrombus, indicating tumor invasion or extension into the venous system.

caption: The clear cell pattern of renal cell carcinoma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of renal cell carcinoma exhibiting a clear cell pattern. The tissue is characterized by nests of tumor cells with distinct clear cytoplasm, separated by a delicate network of thin-walled blood vessels.

caption: Aphthous ulcer. Single ulceration with an erythematous halo sur- rounding a yellowish fibrinopurulent membrane.
summary: The image displays a clinical presentation of an aphthous ulcer located on the oral mucosa, specifically the lower lip or labial mucosa. The lesion is characterized as a single ulceration featuring a central yellowish fibrinopurulent membrane surrounded by a distinct erythematous (red) halo.

caption: Pleomorphic adenoma. (B) High-power view showing epithelial cells as well as myoepi- thelial cells within chondroid matrix material.
summary: The image displays a high-power histological view of a Pleomorphic Adoma, characterized by a mixture of epithelial and myoepithelial cells embedded within a chondroid matrix material.

caption: Pleomorphic adenoma. (A) Low-power view showing a well- demarcated tumor with adjacent, deeply staining, normal salivary gland parenchyma.
summary: The image displays a low-power histological view of a pleomorphic adenoma, characterized by a well-demarcated tumor mass. Adjacent to the tumor is normal salivary gland parenchyma, which appears as a deeply staining region.

caption: Esophagitis. (A) Reflux esophagitis with scattered intraepithelial eosinophils.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the esophagus exhibiting features of reflux esophagitis, characterized by the presence of scattered eosinophils within the epithelial layer.

caption: Esophagitis. (B) Eosinophilic esophagitis with numerous intraepithelial eosinophils.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the esophagus exhibiting features of Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE). The tissue shows a dense infiltration of eosinophils within the epithelial layer, characterized by cells with bilobed nuclei and bright red cytoplasmic granules, indicating an allergic or inflammatory condition.

caption: Esophagitis. (C) Endoscopy reveals circumferential rings in the proximal esophagus of this patient with eosinophilic esophagitis. (Endoscopic image courtesy of Dr. Ira Hanan, The University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: The image is an endoscopic view of the proximal esophagus showing circumferential rings, a characteristic finding in a patient diagnosed with eosinophilic esophagitis.

caption: Barrett esophagus. (C) Histologic appearance of the gastroesophageal junction in Barrett esophagus. Note the transition between esophageal squamous mucosa (lower right) and metaplastic mucosa containing goblet cells (upper). (Endoscopic image courtesy of Dr. Ira Hanan, The University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: The image displays a histologic section of the gastroesophageal junction in Barrett esophagus, showing a transition from esophageal squamous mucosa to metaplastic mucosa containing goblet cells.

caption: Barrett esophagus. (B) Gross image of Barrett esophagus (compare to Fig. 15.7C). Only a few areas of pale squamous mucosa remain within the predominantly metaplastic, reddish mucosa of the distal esophagus.
summary: The image displays a gross specimen of Barrett esophagus, characterized by the replacement of normal squamous epithelium with metaplastic columnar epithelium. The distal esophagus shows predominantly reddish mucosa indicative of intestinal metaplasia, with only sparse areas of pale squamous mucosa remaining.

caption: Barrett esophagus. (A) Narrow band imaging of Barrett esophagus during endoscopy. Note the tan islands of ectopic metaplasia within bluish squamous mucosa.
summary: The image displays a narrow band imaging endoscopic view of Barrett esophagus, characterized by tan-colored islands of ectopic metaplastic tissue situated within the surrounding bluish squamous mucosa.

caption: Esophageal adenocarcinoma. (A) Adenocarcinoma usually occurs distally and, as in this case, often involves the gastric cardia.
summary: The image displays a histological section of esophageal adenocarcinoma, characterized by malignant glandular structures. The caption indicates that this type of cancer typically occurs in the distal esophagus and frequently involves the gastric cardia. The tissue shows neoplastic cells forming irregular glands within a cellular stroma.

caption: Esophageal adenocarcinoma. (B) Esophageal adenocarcinoma growing as back-to-back glands.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an esophagus affected by adenocarcinoma. The caption identifies the condition as esophageal adenocarcinoma, specifically noting the growth pattern of back-to-back glands, which is a characteristic histological feature of this malignancy.

caption: Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. (B) Squamous cell carcinoma composed of nests of malig- nant cells that partially recapitulate the stratified organization of squamous epithelium.
summary: The image displays a histological section of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by nests of malignant cells that partially mimic the stratified organization of normal squamous epithelium.

caption: Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. (A) Squamous cell carci- noma most frequently is found in the mid-esophagus, where it commonly causes strictures.
summary: The image depicts a gross pathology specimen of the esophagus showing squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by a stricture in the mid-esophagus. The tumor appears as a constricted, irregular mass disrupting the normal esophageal lumen.

caption: Peptic ulcer disease. (A) Endoscopic view of typical antral ulcer associated with NSAID use.
summary: The image displays an endoscopic view of a peptic ulcer located in the antrum of the stomach, characterized by a visible lesion with surrounding inflammation and tissue damage, consistent with NSAID-induced peptic ulcer disease.

caption: Peptic ulcer disease. (B) Gross view of a similar ulcer that was resected due to gastric perforation, presenting as free air under the diaphragm. Note the clean edges.
summary: The image displays a gross pathological view of a peptic ulcer, specifically one that was surgically resected due to gastric perforation. The specimen shows a distinct ulceration with clean, well-defined edges, contrasting with the surrounding inflamed tissue.

caption: Peptic ulcer disease. (C) The necrotic ulcer base is composed of granulation tissue overlaid by degraded blood. (Endoscopic image courtesy of Dr. Ira Hanan, The University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of peptic ulcer disease, specifically focusing on the ulcer base. The base is characterized by granulation tissue and is overlaid by degraded blood, indicating the healing or chronic phase of the ulcer.

caption: Hirschsprung disease. (A) Preoperative barium enema study showing constricted rectum (bottom) and dilated sigmoid colon. Ganglion cells were absent in the rectum, but present in the sigmoid colon.
summary: The image is a preoperative barium enema study of a patient with Hirschsprung disease, showing a constricted rectum and a dilated sigmoid colon. The caption indicates that ganglion cells were absent in the rectum but present in the sigmoid colon, which is characteristic of Hirschsprung disease.

caption: Hirschsprung disease. (B) Cor- responding intraoperative appearance of the dilated sigmoid colon.
summary: The image displays the intraoperative appearance of a dilated sigmoid colon, which is a characteristic finding in Hirschsprung disease. The colon appears significantly enlarged and distended.

caption: Celiac disease. (B) Infiltration of the surface epithelium by T lymphocytes, which can be recognized by their densely stained nuclei (labeled T). Compare with elongated, pale-staining epithelial nuclei (labeled E).
summary: The image displays a histological section of the small intestine affected by Celiac disease. It highlights the infiltration of the surface epithelium by T lymphocytes, characterized by their densely stained nuclei, contrasting with the elongated, pale-staining nuclei of the epithelial cells.

caption: Celiac disease. (A) Advanced cases of celiac disease show com- plete loss of villi, or total villous atrophy. Note the dense plasma cell infil- trates in the lamina propria.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the small intestine affected by advanced Celiac disease. The key pathological features include the complete loss of villi (total villous atrophy) and a dense infiltration of plasma cells within the lamina propria.

caption: Clostridium difficile colitis. (A) The colon is coated by tan pseu- domembranes composed of neutrophils, dead epithelial cells, and inflamma- tory debris (endoscopic view).
summary: The image displays an endoscopic view of the colon affected by Clostridium difficile colitis, characterized by the presence of tan pseudomembranes. These pseudomembranes are composed of neutrophils, dead epithelial cells, and inflammatory debris, indicating a severe inflammatory response within the colon.

caption: Clostridium difficile colitis. (B) Typical pattern of neutrophils emanating from a crypt is reminiscent of a volcanic eruption.
summary: The image displays a histological section of colonic tissue affected by Clostridium difficile colitis. The key pathological feature shown is the presence of neutrophils migrating from the crypts into the lumen, creating a pattern described as a 'volcanic eruption'.

caption: Gross pathology of Crohn disease. (A) Small-intestinal stricture.
summary: The image displays gross pathology of Crohn disease, specifically highlighting a small-intestinal stricture. The specimen shows a segment of the small intestine with a narrowed, constricted area, characteristic of the stricture formation seen in Crohn's disease.

caption: Gross pathology of Crohn disease. (B) Linear mucosal ulcers and thickened intestinal wall.
summary: The image displays gross pathology of Crohn disease, characterized by linear mucosal ulcers and a thickened intestinal wall.

caption: Gross pathology of Crohn disease. (C) Creeping fat.
summary: The image displays gross pathology of Crohn disease, specifically highlighting the 'creeping fat' phenomenon, which is characterized by the infiltration of fat into the mesentery and bowel wall.

caption: Microscopic pathology of Crohn disease. (B) Noncaseating granuloma.
summary: The image displays a microscopic pathology slide of Crohn disease, specifically highlighting a noncaseating granuloma. The tissue section reveals a collection of epithelioid histiocytes forming a granuloma, characterized by a central area of fibrinoid necrosis surrounded by a rim of lymphocytes and plasma cells, without the presence of caseous necrosis typical of tuberculosis.

caption: Microscopic pathology of Crohn disease. (A) Haphazard crypt organization results from repeated injury and regeneration.
summary: The image displays a microscopic pathology slide of Crohn disease, characterized by H&E staining. The key pathological feature shown is the haphazard organization of the intestinal crypts, which is a result of repeated cycles of injury and regeneration within the tissue.

caption: Pathology of ulcerative colitis. (A) Endoscopic view of severe ulcerative colitis with ulceration and adherent mucopurulent material.
summary: The image displays an endoscopic view of a severe case of ulcerative colitis, characterized by extensive ulceration and the presence of adherent mucopurulent material on the colonic mucosa.

caption: Pathology of ulcerative colitis. (B) Total colectomy with pancolitis showing active disease, with red, granular mucosa in the cecum (left) and smooth, atrophic mucosa distally (right).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a total colectomy performed for pancolitis, a condition associated with ulcerative colitis. The specimen reveals a contrast in the colonic mucosa: the cecum (left side) exhibits active disease characterized by a red, granular appearance, while the distal colon (right side) shows smooth, atrophic mucosa.

caption: Pathology of ulcerative colitis. (C) Sharp demar- cation between active ulcerative colitis (bottom) and normal (top).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the colon demonstrating the characteristic features of ulcerative colitis. It shows a distinct, sharp demarcation line separating the inflamed, ulcerated mucosa at the bottom from the normal, healthy mucosa at the top.

caption: Pathology of ulcerative colitis. (D) This full-thickness histologic section shows that disease is limited to the mucosa. Compare with Fig. 15.29C. (Endoscopic image courtesy of Dr. Ira Hanan, The University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: The image displays a full-thickness histologic section of tissue affected by ulcerative colitis. The pathology is characterized by inflammation and architectural distortion limited to the mucosa, without involvement of the deeper submucosa or muscularis propria.

caption: Colonic adenomas. (A) Pedunculated adenoma (endoscopic view).
summary: The image displays an endoscopic view of a colonic adenoma, specifically a pedunculated type, characterized by a stalk-like structure supporting a rounded, reddish-brown mass protruding from the colonic mucosa.

caption: Colonic adenomas. (B) Adenoma with a velvety surface.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a colonic adenoma, characterized by a distinct velvety surface texture.

caption: Colonic adenomas. (C) Low-magnification photomicrograph of a pedunculated tubular adenoma.
summary: The image displays a low-magnification photomicrograph of a pedunculated tubular adenoma of the colon, characterized by a stalk-like structure with glandular epithelium.

caption: Colorectal carcinoma. (A) Endoscopic view of ulcerated ascending colon adenocarcinoma.
summary: The image displays an endoscopic view of an ulcerated ascending colon adenocarcinoma, characterized by a dark, irregular mass with surrounding inflamed tissue.

caption: Colorectal carcinoma. (B) Circumferential, ulcerated rectal cancer. Note the anal mucosa at the bottom of the image.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a circumferential, ulcerated rectal cancer. The lesion appears as a large, raised, and irregular mass with central ulceration, encircling the rectal lumen. The surrounding tissue shows the anal mucosa at the distal end.

caption: Colorectal carcinoma. (C) Cancer of the sigmoid colon that has invaded through the muscularis propria and is present within subserosal adipose tissue (left). Areas of chalky necrosis are present within the colon wall (arrow). (Endoscopic image courtesy of Dr. Ira Hanan, The University of Chicago, Chicago, Illinois.)
summary: This endoscopic image of the sigmoid colon demonstrates a carcinoma that has invaded through the muscularis propria into the subserosal adipose tissue. The image highlights areas of chalky necrosis within the colon wall, indicated by an arrow, which is a pathological finding associated with the malignancy.

caption: Histologic appearance of colorectal carcinoma. (A) Well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. Note the elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei. Necrotic debris, present in the gland lumen, is typical.
summary: The image displays a histologic section of colorectal carcinoma, specifically a well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. Key features include elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei within the glandular structures and the presence of necrotic debris within the gland lumens.

caption: Histologic appearance of colorectal carcinoma. (B) Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma forms a few glands but is largely composed of infiltrating nests of tumor cells.
summary: The image displays a histologic section of colorectal carcinoma, specifically a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tissue architecture is characterized by a paucity of glandular structures, with the tumor mass being predominantly composed of infiltrating nests of malignant cells.

caption: Histologic appearance of colorectal carcinoma. (C) Mucinous adenocarcinoma with signet ring cells and extracellular mucin pools.
summary: The image displays a histological section of colorectal carcinoma, specifically a mucinous adenocarcinoma. The tissue architecture is characterized by the presence of signet ring cells and distinct extracellular mucin pools.

caption: Hepatocyte necrosis. In this PAS-D–stained biopsy from a patient with acute hepatitis B, clusters of pigmented hepatocytes with eosinophilic cytoplasm indicate foci of hepatocytes undergoing necrosis. PAS-D, Periodic acid-Schiff stain after diastase digestion.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue from a patient with acute hepatitis B, stained with Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) after diastase digestion. The section reveals clusters of hepatocytes exhibiting eosinophilic cytoplasm and pigmentation, which are identified as foci of hepatocyte necrosis.

caption: Models of liver anatomy. In the lobular model, the terminal hepatic vein is at the center of a “lobule,” while the portal tracts are at the periphery. Pathologists often refer to the regions of the parenchyma as “periportal and centrilobular.” In the acinar model, on the basis of blood flow, three zones can be defined, zone 1 being the closest to the blood supply and zone 3 being the farthest. BD, Bile duct; CV, central hepatic vein; HA, hepatic artery; PV, portal tracts.
summary: The image illustrates two models of liver anatomy: the lobular model, where the terminal hepatic vein is at the center of a lobule and portal tracts are at the periphery, and the acinar model, which defines three zones based on blood flow (zone 1 closest to blood supply, zone 3 farthest).

caption: Hepatocyte apoptosis. This biopsy from a patient with lobular hepatitis due to chronic hepatitis C shows scattered apoptotic hepatocytes (“acidophil bodies”; single arrows) and a patchy inflammatory infiltrate (double arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting hepatocyte apoptosis, characterized by scattered 'acidophil bodies' (apoptotic hepatocytes), and a patchy inflammatory infiltrate, consistent with lobular hepatitis caused by chronic hepatitis C.

caption: Cirrhosis resulting from chronic viral hepatitis. Note the broad scars separating bulging regenerative nodules over the liver surface.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver exhibiting cirrhosis, characterized by a nodular surface with broad fibrous scars separating regenerative nodules, indicative of chronic viral hepatitis.

caption: Alcoholic cirrhosis in an active drinker (A) and following long-term abstinence
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting alcoholic cirrhosis. The tissue is characterized by the presence of regenerative nodules of hepatocytes separated by bands of fibrous tissue (fibrosis). The staining technique used appears to be a trichrome stain, which highlights the collagenous fibrosis in blue against the pink hepatocytes.

caption: Alcoholic cirrhosis in an active drinker (B). A, Thick bands of collagen separate rounded cirrhotic nodules. B, After 1 year of abstinence, most scars are gone (Masson trichrome stain).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting alcoholic cirrhosis, characterized by the presence of thick bands of collagen separating rounded cirrhotic nodules. The tissue is stained using the Masson trichrome stain, which highlights the fibrous tissue.

caption: Potential outcomes of hepatitis B infection in adults, with their approximate frequencies in the United States. *Spontaneous HBsAg clearance occurs during chronic HBV infection at an estimated annual incidence of 1% to 2% in Western countries. As mentioned in the text, fulminant hepatitis and acute hepatic failure are used interchangeably.
summary: The image illustrates the potential outcomes of hepatitis B infection in adults, showing a branching diagram that represents different clinical pathways. The diagram starts with a single outcome (likely representing initial infection) and branches into multiple possible results, including spontaneous HBsAg clearance, chronic HBV infection, fulminant hepatitis, and acute hepatic failure. The caption provides additional context, noting that spontaneous HBsAg clearance occurs at an annual incidence of 1% to 2% in Western countries and that fulminant hepatitis and acute hepatic failure are used interchangeably.

caption: Morphologic features of acute and chronic hepatitis. There is very little portal mononuclear infiltration in acute hepatitis (or sometimes none at all), while in chronic hepatitis portal infiltrates are dense and prominent—the defining feature of chronic hepatitis. Bridging necrosis and fibrosis are shown only for chronic hepatitis, but bridging necrosis may also occur in more severe acute hepatitis. Ductular reactions in chronic hepatitis are minimal in early stages of scarring, but become extensive in late-stage disease.
summary: The image illustrates the morphologic differences between acute and chronic hepatitis. The left panel depicts acute hepatitis, characterized by minimal portal mononuclear infiltration. The right panel shows chronic hepatitis, distinguished by dense and prominent portal infiltrates, bridging necrosis, fibrosis, and extensive ductular reactions.

caption: Ground-glass hepatocytes in chronic hepatitis B, caused by accu- mulation of hepatitis B surface antigen. Hematoxylin-eosin staining shows the presence of abundant, finely granular pink cytoplasmic inclusions; immunostaining (inset) with a specific antibody confirms the presence of surface antigen (brown).
summary: The image displays ground-glass hepatocytes characteristic of chronic hepatitis B, featuring abundant, finely granular pink cytoplasmic inclusions due to the accumulation of hepatitis B surface antigen. Hematoxylin-eosin staining reveals these inclusions, while an inset immunostaining with a specific antibody confirms the presence of surface antigen (brown).

caption: Chronic viral hepatitis due to HCV, showing characteristic portal tract expansion by a dense lymphoid infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting chronic viral hepatitis caused by Hepatitis C Virus (HCV). The key pathological feature shown is the expansion of a portal tract by a dense, nodular lymphoid infiltrate, which is a characteristic finding in HCV infection.

caption: Fatty liver disease associated with chronic alcohol use. A mix of small and large fat droplets (seen as clear vacuoles) is most prominent around the central vein and extends outward to the portal tracts. Some fibrosis (stained blue) is present in a characteristic perisinusoidal “chicken wire fence” pattern (Masson trichrome stain).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue affected by fatty liver disease, likely secondary to chronic alcohol use. The tissue exhibits a mix of small and large fat droplets (appearing as clear vacuoles) within the hepatocytes. A key pathological feature is the presence of fibrosis, stained blue, which forms a characteristic perisinusoidal 'chicken wire fence' pattern surrounding the central vein.

caption: Hepatocyte injury in fatty liver disease associated with chronic alcohol use. (A) Clustered inflammatory cells marking the site of a necrotic hepatocyte. A Mallory-Denk body is present in another hepatocyte (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting hepatocyte injury characteristic of fatty liver disease associated with chronic alcohol use. Key pathological features include a cluster of inflammatory cells surrounding a necrotic hepatocyte and the presence of a Mallory-Denk body within another hepatocyte.

caption: Hepatocyte injury in fatty liver disease associated with chronic alcohol use. (B) “Ballooned” hepatocytes (arrowheads) associated with clusters of inflammatory cells. The inset stained for keratins 8 and 18 (brown) shows a ballooned cell (dotted line) in which keratins have been ubiquitinylated and have collapsed into an immunoreactive Mallory-Denk body, leaving the cytoplasm “empty.”
summary: The image displays hepatocyte injury in fatty liver disease associated with chronic alcohol use, characterized by ballooned hepatocytes and clusters of inflammatory cells. The inset shows an immunohistochemical stain for keratins 8 and 18, highlighting a ballooned cell with a Mallory-Denk body formed by ubiquitinylated keratins.

caption: Hereditary hemochromatosis. In this Prussian blue–stained section, hepatocellular iron appears blue. The parenchymal architecture is normal at this stage of disease, even with such abundant iron.
summary: This is a micrograph of liver tissue (parenchyma) from a patient with hereditary hemochromatosis. The image utilizes a Prussian blue stain which highlights the presence of abnormal amounts of intracellular iron within the hepatocytes (blue staining). Despite the abundant iron deposition, the underlying tissue architecture remains intact.

caption: α1-Anti-trypsin deficiency. Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain after diastase digestion of the liver, highlights the characteristic magenta cytoplas- mic granules.
summary: The image displays a liver tissue section stained with Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) after diastase digestion, revealing characteristic magenta cytoplasmic granules indicative of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

caption: Acute large-duct obstruction with ascending cholangitis. Super- imposed on features of duct obstruction (edema, ductular reaction) is an infiltrate of neutrophils involving the bile duct, the hallmark of ascending cholangitis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a bile duct exhibiting acute large-duct obstruction. Key features include ductal edema, a ductular reaction, and a significant neutrophilic infiltrate within the duct wall, which is characteristic of ascending cholangitis.

caption: Cirrhosis secondary to primary biliary cholangitis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting cirrhosis, characterized by the presence of fibrous bands and regenerative nodules, which are secondary to primary biliary cholangitis.

caption: Neonatal hepatitis. Note the multinucleated giant hepatocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue from a neonate, identified as neonatal hepatitis. The key pathological feature highlighted is the presence of multinucleated giant hepatocytes, which are large cells containing multiple nuclei, surrounded by other hepatocytes and inflammatory cells.

caption: Primary biliary cirrhosis. A portal tract is markedly expanded by an infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells. Note the granulomatous reac- tion to the bile duct undergoing destruction (the “florid duct lesion”).
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting features of Primary Biliary Cirrhosis. The portal tract is significantly expanded by a dense infiltrate of inflammatory cells, specifically lymphocytes and plasma cells. A key pathological feature visible is the florid duct lesion, characterized by a granulomatous reaction surrounding and destroying a bile duct.

caption: Imaging studies of a patient with primary sclerosing cholangitis. (B) Endoscopic retrograde cholangiography of the same patient shows nearly identical features as in A.
summary: The image displays an endoscopic retrograde cholangiography (ERC) of a patient with primary sclerosing cholangitis, showing the biliary tree with characteristic features such as strictures and dilations.

caption: Imaging studies of a patient with primary sclerosing cholangitis. (A) Magnetic resonance cholangiography of the bile ducts shows focal dila- tion in some ducts (bright, broad areas) and stricturing of others (thinning or absence).
summary: The image displays a Magnetic Resonance Cholangiography (MRC) scan of a patient diagnosed with Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC). The scan visualizes the biliary tree, highlighting characteristic features of the disease including focal dilation of certain bile ducts appearing as bright, broad areas, and stricturing of other ducts manifesting as thinning or absence of the ductal lumen.

caption: Primary sclerosing cholangitis. A bile duct undergoing degenera- tion is entrapped in a dense, “onion-skin” concentric scar.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue affected by Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC). The central feature is a bile duct exhibiting degeneration, which is surrounded by a dense, concentric fibrosis pattern resembling an 'onion-skin' layer.

caption: Liver infarct. A thrombus is lodged in a peripheral branch of the hepatic artery (arrow) and compresses the adjacent portal vein; the distal hepatic tissue is pale, with a hemorrhagic margin.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver exhibiting an infarct. A thrombus is lodged in a peripheral branch of the hepatic artery, compressing the adjacent portal vein, resulting in pale distal hepatic tissue with a hemorrhagic margin.

caption: Budd-Chiari syndrome. Thrombosis of the major hepatic veins has caused severe hepatic congestion.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver affected by Budd-Chiari syndrome, characterized by severe hepatic congestion due to thrombosis of the major hepatic veins. The liver exhibits a dark, congested appearance with areas of hemorrhage and potential necrosis, indicative of the syndrome's impact on liver function and structure.

caption: Cholesterol gallstones. The wall of the gallbladder is thickened and fibrotic due to chronic cholecystitis.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a gallbladder containing multiple cholesterol gallstones. The gallbladder wall exhibits thickening and fibrosis, indicative of chronic cholecystitis.

caption: Pigment gallstones. Several faceted black gallstones are present in this otherwise unremarkable gallbladder from a patient with a mechanical mitral valve prosthesis, leading to chronic intravascular hemolysis.
summary: The image displays a gallbladder containing multiple faceted black pigment gallstones, which are associated with chronic intravascular hemolysis caused by a mechanical mitral valve prosthesis.

caption: Chronic cholecystitis. (A) The gallbladder mucosa is infiltrated by chronic inflammatory cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the gallbladder mucosa affected by chronic cholecystitis, characterized by the infiltration of chronic inflammatory cells within the tissue.

caption: Chronic cholecystitis. (B) A Rokitansky-Aschoff sinus containing a fragmented bile pigment stone.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the gallbladder wall exhibiting chronic cholecystitis. A prominent feature is a Rokitansky-Aschoff sinus, which is a deep invagination of the mucosa into the muscularis layer, containing a fragmented bile pigment stone.

caption: Pancreatic pseudocyst. (A) Cross-section revealing a poorly defined cyst with a necrotic brownish wall.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a pancreatic pseudocyst. It shows a cross-section of the organ revealing a poorly defined cystic cavity surrounded by a thick, necrotic, brownish wall.

caption: Pancreatic pseudocyst. (B) Histologically, the cyst lacks an epithelial lining and instead is lined by fibrin and granulation tissue, with typical changes of chronic inflammation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pancreatic pseudocyst, characterized by a cystic space lacking an epithelial lining. Instead, the cyst is lined by fibrin and granulation tissue, accompanied by typical signs of chronic inflammation.
