caption: Mucinous cystic neoplasm. (A) Cross-section through a mucinous multiloculated cyst in the tail of the pancreas. The cysts are large and filled with tenacious mucin.
summary: The image displays a histological cross-section of a mucinous cystic neoplasm located in the tail of the pancreas. The tissue architecture is characterized by large, multiloculated cystic spaces that are filled with tenacious mucin, surrounded by cellular tissue.

caption: Mucinous cystic neoplasm. (B) The cysts are lined by columnar mucinous epithe- lium, with a densely cellular “ovarian” stroma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a mucinous cystic neoplasm, characterized by cysts lined by columnar mucinous epithelium and underlain by a densely cellular 'ovarian-type' stroma.

caption: Carcinoma of the pancreas. (A) Cross-section through the head of the pancreas and adjacent common bile duct showing both an ill-defined mass in the pancreatic substance (arrowheads) and the green discoloration of the duct resulting from total obstruction of bile flow.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the pancreas head, revealing an ill-defined carcinoma mass within the pancreatic tissue and green discoloration of the adjacent common bile duct due to total obstruction of bile flow.

caption: Carcinoma in situ (Bowen disease) of the penis. The epithelium above the intact basement membrane shows delayed maturation and disor- ganization (left). Higher magnification (right) shows several mitotic figures, some above the basal layer, and nuclear pleomorphism.
summary: The image displays histological sections of the penis exhibiting carcinoma in situ, specifically Bowen disease. The left panel illustrates the full-thickness epithelium showing disorganization and delayed maturation while remaining above an intact basement membrane. The right panel provides a higher magnification view revealing cellular abnormalities including mitotic figures located above the basal layer and nuclear pleomorphism.

caption: Carcinoma of the penis. The glans penis is deformed by an ulcer- ated, infiltrative mass.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a penis affected by carcinoma. The glans penis is visibly deformed by a large, ulcerated, and infiltrative mass, indicating an advanced stage of the disease.

caption: Seminoma of the testis appearing as a well-circumscribed, pale, fleshy, homogeneous mass.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a testis affected by a seminoma. The mass is characterized as well-circumscribed, pale, fleshy, and homogeneous, indicating a distinct tumor growth within the organ.

caption: Seminoma of the testis. Microscopic examination reveals large cells with distinct cell borders, pale nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and a sparse lymphocytic infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a testicular seminoma, characterized by sheets of large tumor cells with distinct cell borders, pale nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, accompanied by a sparse lymphocytic infiltrate.

caption: Embryonal carcinoma. In contrast with the seminoma in Fig. 18.3, this tumor is hemorrhagic.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an embryonal carcinoma, characterized by a large, hemorrhagic mass. The caption contrasts this appearance with that of a seminoma, noting the significant hemorrhagic nature of the embryonal carcinoma.

caption: Embryonal carcinoma. Note the sheets of undifferentiated cells and primitive glandlike structures. The nuclei are large and hyperchromatic.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Embryonal carcinoma, characterized by sheets of undifferentiated cells and primitive gland-like structures. The nuclei are notably large and hyperchromatic.

caption: Yolk sac tumor demonstrating areas of loosely textured, micro- cystic tissue and papillary structures resembling a developing glomerulus (Schiller-Duval bodies).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Yolk sac tumor, characterized by loosely textured, micro-cystic tissue and papillary structures that resemble a developing glomerulus, known as Schiller-Duval bodies.

caption: Choriocarcinoma. Both cytotrophoblastic cells with single central nuclei (arrowhead, upper-right) and syncytiotrophoblastic cells with multiple dark nuclei embedded in eosinophilic cytoplasm (arrow, middle) are present. Hemorrhage and necrosis are prominent.
summary: The image displays a histological section of choriocarcinoma, characterized by the presence of distinct cytotrophoblastic cells and syncytiotrophoblastic cells. The tissue exhibits significant hemorrhage and necrosis.

caption: Nodular prostatic hyperplasia. Well-defined nodules compress the urethra into a slitlike lumen.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a prostate gland exhibiting nodular hyperplasia. The tissue shows multiple distinct nodules that have expanded and compressed the urethra, resulting in a narrowed, slit-like lumen.

caption: Nodular hyperplasia of the prostate. (A) Low-power photomicrograph demonstrates a well-demarcated nodule at the right of the field, with a portion of urethra seen to the left. In other cases of nodular hyperplasia, the nodularity is caused predominantly by stromal, rather than glandular, prolifera- tion.
summary: The image is a low-power photomicrograph of nodular hyperplasia of the prostate, showing a well-demarcated nodule composed predominantly of glandular proliferation with some stromal elements. A portion of the urethra is visible to the left of the nodule.

caption: Nodular hyperplasia of the prostate. (B) Higher-power photomicrograph demonstrates the morphology of the hyperplastic glands, which are large and have papillary infoldings.
summary: The image displays a higher-power photomicrograph of nodular hyperplasia of the prostate, characterized by large hyperplastic glands exhibiting papillary infoldings.

caption: Adenocarcinoma of the prostate. Carcinomatous tissue is seen on the posterior aspect (lower left). Note the solid whiter tissue of cancer, in contrast with the spongy appearance of the benign peripheral zone on the contralateral side.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a prostate gland with adenocarcinoma. The cancerous tissue is located on the posterior aspect (lower left) and appears as solid, whiter tissue, contrasting with the spongy appearance of the benign peripheral zone on the contralateral side.

caption: Cystoscopic appearance of a papillary urothelial tumor, resem- bling coral, within the bladder.
summary: The image displays a cystoscopic view of a papillary urothelial tumor within the bladder, characterized by its coral-like appearance.

caption: Noninvasive low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma. Higher magnification (right) shows slightly irregular nuclei with scattered mitotic figures (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of noninvasive low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma. The left side shows the overall papillary architecture of the tumor, while the right side provides a higher magnification view revealing cellular details, specifically slightly irregular nuclei and scattered mitotic figures indicated by an arrow.

caption: Carcinoma in situ (CIS) with enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei and a mitotic figure (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue exhibiting Carcinoma in situ (CIS). The epithelium is characterized by a full-thickness proliferation of atypical cells with enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei. A mitotic figure is identified within the epithelial layer, indicated by an arrow, signifying active cell division.

caption: Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Gram stain of urethral discharge demon- strates characteristic gram-negative, intracellular diplococci (arrow).
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a Gram stain of urethral discharge, revealing characteristic Gram-negative, intracellular diplococci, which are indicative of Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection.

caption: Acute epididymitis caused by gonococcal infection. The epididy- mis is involved by an abscess. Normal testis is seen on the right.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a testis and epididymis. The left side shows the epididymis involved by a large, circumscribed abscess containing yellowish-white purulent material. The right side of the specimen shows the normal testis, which appears distinct from the inflamed epididymis. The caption identifies the condition as acute epididymitis caused by a gonococcal infection.

caption: Nonneoplastic vulvar epithelial disorders. (A) Lichen sclerosus. There is marked thinning of the epidermis, fibrosis of the superficial dermis, and chronic inflammatory cells in the deeper dermis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin, specifically the vulva, demonstrating features of Lichen Sclerosus. Key pathological findings include marked thinning of the epidermis (atrophy), fibrosis of the superficial dermis, and a presence of chronic inflammatory cells within the deeper dermis.

caption: Nonneoplastic vulvar epithelial disorders. (B) Lichen simplex chronicus displaying thickened epidermis and hyperkeratosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin, specifically the vulva, exhibiting features of Lichen Simplex Chronicus. The tissue shows significant thickening of the epidermis (acanthosis) and a thickened stratum corneum (hyperkeratosis).

caption: Paget disease of the vulva. Large tumor cells with pale-pink cyto- plasm are seen infiltrating the epidermis. Chronic inflammatory cells are present in the underlying dermis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Paget disease of the vulva, characterized by large tumor cells with pale-pink cytoplasm infiltrating the epidermis, accompanied by chronic inflammatory cells in the underlying dermis.

caption: Cervical transformation zone showing the transition from mature glycogenated squamous epithelium, to immature metaplastic squamous cells, to columnar endocervical glandular epithelium.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the cervical transformation zone, illustrating the distinct transition between different epithelial types. It shows mature glycogenated squamous epithelium at the top, transitioning into immature metaplastic squamous cells, and finally to columnar endocervical glandular epithelium at the bottom.

caption: Spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) with normal squamous epithelium for comparison: LSIL with koilocytotic atypia; HSIL with progressive atypia in all layers of the epithelium; and HSIL with diffuse atypia and loss of maturation (carcinoma in situ, far right image).
summary: The image displays a histological section illustrating the spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL). It features a comparison between normal squamous epithelium and pathological states, specifically Low-grade Squamous Intraepithelial Lesion (LSIL) characterized by koilocytotic atypia, High-grade Squamous Intraepithelial Lesion (HSIL) showing progressive atypia throughout the epithelial layers, and Carcinoma in situ exhibiting diffuse atypia with a loss of maturation.

caption: Spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) with normal squamous epithelium for comparison: LSIL with koilocytotic atypia; HSIL with progressive atypia in all layers of the epithelium; and HSIL with diffuse atypia and loss of maturation (carcinoma in situ, far right image).
summary: The image displays a histological section illustrating the spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL), ranging from low-grade (LSIL) to high-grade (HSIL) and carcinoma in situ. It features a comparison with normal squamous epithelium, highlighting specific pathological changes such as koilocytotic atypia in LSIL, progressive atypia in HSIL, and diffuse atypia with loss of maturation in carcinoma in situ.

caption: Spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) with normal squamous epithelium for comparison: LSIL with koilocytotic atypia; HSIL with progressive atypia in all layers of the epithelium; and HSIL with diffuse atypia and loss of maturation (carcinoma in situ, far right image).
summary: The image displays a histological section illustrating the spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL), ranging from normal squamous epithelium on the left to low-grade SIL (LSIL) showing koilocytotic atypia, high-grade SIL (HSIL) with progressive atypia throughout the epithelium, and carcinoma in situ characterized by diffuse atypia and loss of maturation on the far right.

caption: Spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) with normal squamous epithelium for comparison: LSIL with koilocytotic atypia; HSIL with progressive atypia in all layers of the epithelium; and HSIL with diffuse atypia and loss of maturation (carcinoma in situ, far right image).
summary: The image displays a histological spectrum of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL), ranging from normal squamous epithelium on the left to low-grade SIL (LSIL) showing koilocytotic atypia, high-grade SIL (HSIL) with progressive atypia throughout the epithelium, and carcinoma in situ characterized by diffuse atypia and loss of maturation on the far right.

caption: Cervical os with surrounding, invasive, exophytic cervical carcinoma.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a cervix, specifically focusing on the cervical os. The specimen reveals a large, fleshy, exophytic mass that is invading the surrounding tissue, consistent with the diagnosis of invasive cervical carcinoma.

caption: Ovarian endometriosis. Sectioning of ovary shows a large endo- metriotic cyst with degenerated blood (“chocolate cyst”).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an ovary affected by endometriosis. The sectioning reveals a large cystic structure filled with dark, degenerated blood, characteristic of a 'chocolate cyst' associated with endometriosis.

caption: Uterine leiomyomas. (B) Microscopic appearance of leiomyoma shows bundles of normal-looking smooth muscle cells.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a uterine leiomyoma, characterized by bundles of smooth muscle cells that appear normal in morphology.

caption: Uterine leiomyomas. (A) The uterus is opened to show multiple submucosal, myometrial, and subserosal gray-white tumors, each with a charac- teristic whorled appearance on cut section.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a uterus that has been opened to reveal multiple uterine leiomyomas (fibroids). These tumors are located in the submucosal, myometrial, and subserosal layers. On the cut surface, they exhibit a characteristic gray-white color and a distinct whorled appearance.

caption: Pelvic inflammatory disease, bilateral and asymmetric. The tube and ovary to the left of the uterus are totally obscured by a hemorrhagic inflammatory mass. The tube is adherent to the adjacent ovary on the other side.
summary: The image depicts a gross pathology specimen of the female reproductive system, specifically showing bilateral and asymmetric pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). The left side shows a hemorrhagic inflammatory mass that obscures the tube and ovary, while the right side shows adhesion between the tube and ovary.

caption: Mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst) of the ovary. A ball of hair (bottom) and a mixture of tissues are evident.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst) of the ovary. The specimen reveals a large cystic structure containing a prominent mass of hair and a heterogeneous mixture of other tissues.

caption: Complete hydatidiform mole, consisting of numerous swollen (hydropic) villi.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a complete hydatidiform mole, characterized by numerous swollen (hydropic) chorionic villi. The villi appear edematous and are lined by trophoblastic cells, with no fetal tissue present, which is typical for a complete mole.

caption: Complete hydatidiform mole. In this microscopic image, dis- tended hydropic villi (below) and proliferation of the chorionic epithelium (above) are evident.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a complete hydatidiform mole, characterized by the presence of distended hydropic villi and a proliferation of the chorionic epithelium.

caption: Choriocarcinoma. This field contains both neoplastic cytotro- phoblast and multinucleate syncytiotrophoblast.
summary: The image displays a histological section of choriocarcinoma, characterized by a mixture of neoplastic cytotrophoblasts and multinucleated syncytiotrophoblasts. The tissue shows areas of hemorrhage and necrosis, with the neoplastic cells exhibiting varying degrees of atypia and mitotic activity.

caption: Intralobular stromal neoplasms. (A) Fibroadenoma. This benign tumor has an expansile growth pattern with pushing circumscribed borders.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Fibroadenoma, identified as an intralobular stromal neoplasm. It features a benign tumor characterized by an expansile growth pattern and distinct, pushing circumscribed borders, with visible glandular structures embedded within a fibrous stroma.

caption: Intralobular stromal neoplasms. (B) Phyllodes tumors. Proliferating stromal cells distort the glandular tissue, forming cleftlike spaces, and bulge into surrounding stroma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Phyllodes tumor, an intralobular stromal neoplasm. The visual evidence shows a biphasic tumor composed of proliferating stromal cells that distort the glandular tissue, creating characteristic cleft-like spaces and bulging into the surrounding stroma.

caption: Benign epithelial breast disease. (B) Proliferative breast disease is characterized by increased numbers of epithelial cells, as in this example of epithelial hyperplasia.
summary: The image displays a histological section of breast tissue demonstrating benign epithelial disease, specifically epithelial hyperplasia. The visual evidence shows a ductal structure with a significant increase in the number of epithelial cells lining the lumen, resulting in a thickened epithelial layer and a reduced lumen size, consistent with the caption's description of proliferative breast disease.

caption: Benign epithelial breast disease. (C) Proliferative breast disease with atypia. The proliferating epithelial cells are monomorphic in appearance and pile up to form abnormal architectural structures.
summary: The image displays a histological section of breast tissue exhibiting benign epithelial disease, specifically a proliferative breast disease with atypia. The visual evidence shows a distinct nodule or lesion composed of a dense proliferation of monomorphic epithelial cells that have piled up to create an abnormal architectural structure, surrounded by fibrous stroma.

caption: Benign epithelial breast disease. (A) Nonproliferative disease. An apocrine cyst is shown that is a common feature of nonproliferative breast disease.
summary: The image displays a histological section of breast tissue, specifically illustrating a benign epithelial breast disease. The primary feature shown is an apocrine cyst, which is identified as a common characteristic of nonproliferative breast disease.

caption: Major pathways of breast cancer development. The most common pathway (yellow arrow) leads to ER-positive cancers. Morphologically recognized precursor lesions include flat epithelial atypia, ADH, and DCIS, all of which share certain genomic events with invasive ER-positive carcinomas, such gains of chromosome 1, losses of chromosome 16, and mutations of PIK3CA (the gene encoding PI3K). By gene expression profiling, these cancers are classified as “luminal.” This is the type of cancer that arises most commonly in individuals with germline BRCA2 mutations. Less common are cancers that overexpress HER2 because of gene amplification (green arrow). These cancers may be positive or negative for ER and are usually associated with germline TP53 mutations. A possible precursor lesion is atypical apocrine adenosis, which shares features with apocrine DCIS. The least common but molecularly most distinctive type of breast cancer is negative for ER and HER2 (“triple negative”; blue arrow). These cancers have loss of BRCA1 and TP53 function and are genomically unstable. The majority of triple-negative cancers are classified as “basal-like” by gene expression profiling.
summary: The image illustrates the major pathways of breast cancer development, highlighting three distinct molecular subtypes: ER-positive (luminal) cancers, HER2-overexpressing cancers, and triple-negative (basal-like) cancers. Each pathway includes precursor lesions and associated genetic alterations, with ER-positive cancers being the most common and linked to BRCA2 mutations, HER2-overexpressing cancers associated with TP53 mutations, and triple-negative cancers characterized by BRCA1 and TP53 dysfunction.

caption: Carcinoma in situ. (A) Lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS).
summary: The image displays a histological section of breast tissue stained with H&E, illustrating Lobular Carcinoma In Situ (LCIS). The key feature is the proliferation of small, uniform, round-to-oval cells filling and distending the acini of the lobules. These cells are characterized by a lack of cohesion, often described as having a 'targetoid' or 'swiss cheese' appearance due to the preservation of the lobular architecture despite the cellular expansion. The surrounding stroma appears compressed.

caption: Carcinoma in situ. (B) Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS). DCIS partially involves the lobule in the lower half of this photo and has completely effaced the upper lobules, producing a ductlike appearance.
summary: The image displays a histological section of breast tissue demonstrating Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS). The caption identifies the pathology as DCIS, noting that it partially involves the lower lobule and completely effaces the upper lobules, resulting in a solid, duct-like appearance characterized by the proliferation of neoplastic cells.

caption: Carcinoma in situ. (C) Mammographic detection of calcifications associated with DCIS.
summary: The image displays a mammogram showing a cluster of calcifications within the breast tissue, which is indicative of Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS). The caption identifies this as a mammographic detection of calcifications associated with DCIS.

caption: Normal anterior pituitary gland. The gland is populated by several distinct cell types that express different peptide hormones. These hormones are basophilic (blue), eosinophilic (red), or nonstaining in routine sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin, which allows the various cell types to be identified. Note also the presence of a fine reticulin network between the cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a normal anterior pituitary gland. It reveals a cellular population composed of distinct cell types characterized by their staining properties: basophilic (blue), eosinophilic (red), and nonstaining cells. The tissue architecture is supported by a fine reticulin network.

caption: Pituitary adenoma. This massive, nonfunctioning adenoma has grown far beyond the confines of the sella turcica and has distorted the overlying brain. Nonfunctioning adenomas tend to be larger at the time of diagnosis than those that secrete a hormone.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain with a large, nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma. The tumor is described as 'massive' and has grown significantly beyond the sella turcica, causing distortion of the overlying brain structures.

caption: Pituitary adenoma. The monomorphism of these cells contrasts with the admixture of cells seen in the normal anterior pituitary gland in Fig. 20.1. Note also the absence of the reticulin network.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pituitary adenoma, characterized by a monotonous population of cells and the notable absence of a reticulin network, contrasting with the cellular diversity found in normal anterior pituitary tissue.

caption: A patient with hyperthyroidism. A wide-eyed, staring gaze, caused by overactivity of the sympathetic nervous system, is one of the classic features of this disorder. In Graves disease, one of the most important causes of hyperthyroidism, accumulation of loose connective tissue behind the orbits also adds to the protuberant appearance of the eyes.
summary: The image depicts a patient with hyperthyroidism, specifically Graves' disease, characterized by a wide-eyed, staring gaze due to sympathetic nervous system overactivity and accumulation of loose connective tissue behind the orbits, contributing to the protuberant appearance of the eyes.

caption: Hashimoto thyroiditis. The thyroid parenchyma contains a dense lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centers. Residual thyroid follicles lined by deeply eosinophilic Hürthle cells also are seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of thyroid tissue affected by Hashimoto thyroiditis. It features a dense lymphocytic infiltrate containing germinal centers and residual thyroid follicles lined by Hürthle cells.

caption: Graves disease. The follicles are lined by tall columnar epithelial cells that are actively resorbing the colloid in the centers of the follicles, resulting in a “scalloped” appearance of the colloid.
summary: The image displays a histological section of thyroid tissue affected by Graves disease. The follicles are lined by tall columnar epithelial cells that are actively resorbing the colloid in the centers of the follicles, resulting in a 'scalloped' appearance of the colloid.

caption: Multinodular goiter. (A) Gross morphologic appearance. The coarsely nodular gland contains areas of fibrosis and cystic change.
summary: The image displays the gross morphologic appearance of a multinodular goiter, characterized by a coarsely nodular gland with visible areas of fibrosis and cystic change.

caption: Multinodular goiter. (B) Photomicro- graph of a specimen from a hyperplastic nodule, with compressed residual thyroid parenchyma on the periphery. The hyperplastic follicles contain abundant pink “colloid” within their lumina. Note the absence of a prominent capsule, a feature distinguishing such lesions from neoplasms of the thyroid. (B, Courtesy of Dr. William Westra, Department of Pathology, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, Maryland.)
summary: The image displays a photomicrograph of a hyperplastic nodule from a multinodular goiter, characterized by abundant pink colloid within the lumina of hyperplastic follicles and compressed residual thyroid parenchyma on the periphery. A key distinguishing feature noted is the absence of a prominent capsule, which helps differentiate this benign lesion from thyroid neoplasms.

caption: Hürthle cell adenoma. On this high-power view, the tumor is composed of cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and small regular nuclei.
summary: The image displays a high-power microscopic view of a Hürthle cell adenoma, characterized by a tumor composed of cells featuring abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and small, regular nuclei.

caption: Papillary carcinoma of the thyroid gland. (A to C) A papillary carcinoma with grossly discernible papillary structures. In this particular example, well-formed papillae (B) are lined by cells with characteristic empty-appearing nuclei, sometimes termed Orphan Annie eye nuclei (C). (D) Cells obtained by fine-needle aspiration of a papillary carcinoma. Characteristic intranuclear inclusions are visible in some of the aspirated cells (arrows).
summary: The image displays a histological section of papillary carcinoma of the thyroid gland, characterized by well-formed papillary structures lined by cells with distinctive 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei. Additionally, fine-needle aspiration cytology reveals cells with intranuclear inclusions, which are diagnostic features of this malignancy.

caption: Papillary carcinoma of the thyroid gland. (A to C) A papillary carcinoma with grossly discernible papillary structures. In this particular example, well-formed papillae (B) are lined by cells with characteristic empty-appearing nuclei, sometimes termed Orphan Annie eye nuclei (C). (D) Cells obtained by fine-needle aspiration of a papillary carcinoma. Characteristic intranuclear inclusions are visible in some of the aspirated cells (arrows).
summary: The image depicts a papillary carcinoma of the thyroid gland, characterized by well-formed papillary structures lined by cells with characteristic empty-appearing nuclei (Orphan Annie eye nuclei). Additionally, fine-needle aspiration reveals cells with intranuclear inclusions, which are diagnostic features of this malignancy.

caption: Follicular carcinoma of the thyroid gland. A few of the glandular lumina contain recognizable colloid.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a thyroid gland affected by follicular carcinoma. The tissue architecture is characterized by neoplastic follicles of varying sizes. Some of these glandular lumina contain eosinophilic colloid, while others appear empty or contain scanty colloid. The cells forming the follicles are arranged in a solid or trabecular pattern in areas, lacking a true capsule or showing capsular invasion, which is a key feature of follicular carcinoma.

caption: Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid gland. These tumors typically contain amyloid, visible here as homogeneous extracellular material, derived from calcitonin molecules secreted by the neoplastic cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Medullary carcinoma of the thyroid gland. The tissue architecture is characterized by neoplastic cells arranged in nests or trabeculae, separated by a distinct, homogeneous extracellular material identified as amyloid. This amyloid deposition is a key pathological feature of this tumor type.

caption: Technetium-99 radionuclide scan demonstrates an area of increased uptake corresponding to the left inferior parathyroid gland (arrow). This proved to be a parathyroid adenoma. Preoperative scintigraphy is useful in localizing and distinguishing adenomas from parathyroid hyperplasia, in which more than one gland will demonstrate increased uptake.
summary: The image is a Technetium-99 radionuclide scan of the neck showing an area of increased uptake in the left inferior parathyroid gland, which was confirmed to be a parathyroid adenoma. The caption explains that preoperative scintigraphy helps localize and differentiate adenomas from parathyroid hyperplasia.

caption: Chief cell parathyroid adenoma. (A) In this low-power view, a solitary hypercellular adenoma is delineated from the residual normocellular gland on the upper right.
summary: The image displays a low-power histological view of a parathyroid adenoma. It shows a distinct boundary separating a hypercellular adenoma from the adjacent residual normocellular parathyroid gland tissue.

caption: Chief cell parathyroid adenoma. (B) High-power detail shows minimal variation in nuclear size and occasional follicle formation.
summary: The image displays a high-power microscopic view of a parathyroid adenoma, specifically composed of chief cells. The tissue exhibits a monotonous population of cells with minimal variation in nuclear size and occasional follicle formation.

caption: (B) Amyloidosis of a pancreatic islet in type 2 diabetes. Amyloidosis typically is observed late in the natural history of this form of diabetes, with islet inflammation noted at earlier observations.
summary: The image depicts a histological section of a pancreatic islet affected by amyloidosis, a condition commonly associated with type 2 diabetes. The caption indicates that amyloidosis typically appears in the later stages of type 2 diabetes, following earlier observations of islet inflammation.

caption: (A) Autoimmune insulitis in a human pancreatic explant. Arrows point to inflammation surrounding islet of Langerhans, while the surrounding acinar structures are essentially normal. (Photograph provided by Dr. Martha Campbell-Thompson, JDRF Network for Pancreatic Organ Donors, University of Florida., Gainesville, Florida.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of human pancreatic tissue exhibiting autoimmune insulitis, characterized by inflammatory cell infiltration surrounding the islets of Langerhans, while the adjacent acinar structures remain largely unaffected.

caption: Severe renal hyaline arteriolosclerosis in a periodic acid–Schiff stained specimen. Note the markedly thickened, tortuous afferent arteriole. The amorphous nature of the thickened vascular wall is evident.
summary: The image displays a histological section of kidney tissue exhibiting severe renal hyaline arteriolosclerosis. The specimen is stained with Periodic Acid-Schiff (PAS), highlighting the amorphous, eosinophilic (pink) thickening of the vascular wall of an afferent arteriole, which appears markedly thickened and tortuous.

caption: Nodular glomerulosclerosis in a renal specimen from a patient with long-standing diabetes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney, specifically highlighting a glomerulus exhibiting nodular glomerulosclerosis. This pathological finding is characteristic of diabetic nephropathy, resulting from long-standing diabetes mellitus. The glomerular tuft shows distinct nodular formations, likely Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules, surrounded by expanded mesangial areas and thickened capillary loops.

caption: Renal glomerulus showing markedly thickened glomerular base- ment membrane (B) in a diabetic. L, Glomerular capillary lumen; U, urinary space.
summary: Transmission electron micrograph of a renal glomerulus from a diabetic patient, demonstrating marked thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (B). The image also shows the glomerular capillary lumen (L) and the urinary space (U).

caption: Nodular glomerulosclerosis in a renal specimen from a patient with long-standing diabetes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney, specifically focusing on a glomerulus exhibiting nodular glomerulosclerosis. This pathological finding is characteristic of diabetic nephropathy, evidenced by the presence of a large, distinct nodular lesion within the glomerular tuft, surrounded by compressed capillary loops and interstitial tissue.

caption: Nephrosclerosis in a patient with long-standing diabetes. The bisected kidney demonstrates diffuse granular transformation of the surface (left) and marked thinning of the cortex (right). Additional features include some irregular depressions, the result of pyelonephritis, and an incidental cortical cyst (far right).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney bisected to reveal pathological changes associated with nephrosclerosis in a patient with long-standing diabetes. The specimen shows a diffuse granular transformation of the kidney surface and marked thinning of the cortex. Additional features include irregular depressions resulting from pyelonephritis and an incidental cortical cyst.

caption: Characteristic morphologic changes of diabetic retinopathy. Fea- tures include advanced proliferative retinopathy with retinal hemorrhages, exudates, neovascularization, and tractional retinal detachment (lower-right corner).
summary: The image displays characteristic morphologic changes of diabetic retinopathy, specifically advanced proliferative retinopathy. Key features visible include retinal hemorrhages, exudates, neovascularization, and a tractional retinal detachment located in the lower-right corner.

caption: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (PanNET), also called islet cell tumor. The neoplastic cells are monotonous in appearance and demonstrate minimal pleomorphism or mitotic activity. There is abundant amyloid deposi- tion, characteristic of an insulinoma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor (PanNET), also known as an islet cell tumor. The neoplastic cells are characterized by a monotonous appearance with minimal pleomorphism or mitotic activity. A key feature visible is the abundant amyloid deposition, which is characteristic of an insulinoma.

caption: Diffuse hyperplasia of the adrenal gland (bottom) contrasted with a normal adrenal gland (top). In a cross-section, the adrenal cortex is yellow and thickened, and a subtle nodularity is evident. The abnormal gland was from a patient with ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome, in whom both adrenal glands were diffusely hyperplastic. ACTH, Adrenocorticotropic hormone.
summary: The image displays a comparison between a normal adrenal gland (top) and an adrenal gland exhibiting diffuse hyperplasia (bottom). The hyperplastic gland shows a yellow, thickened adrenal cortex with subtle nodularity, indicative of ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome.

caption: (A) Primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical hyperplasia showing prominent pigmented nodules in the adrenal gland.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an adrenal gland exhibiting Primary Pigmented Nodular Adrenocortical Hyperplasia (PPNAH). The gland shows multiple prominent, darkly pigmented nodules scattered throughout the tissue, which is characteristic of this condition. A metric scale is included for size reference.

caption: (B) On histologic examina- tion, the nodules are composed of cells containing lipofuscin pigment, seen in the right part of the field. (Photographs courtesy of Dr. Aidan Carney, Department of Medicine, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota.)
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue, likely liver, showing a distinct transition between two areas. The left side features normal-appearing parenchymal cells with pale cytoplasm and distinct nuclei. The right side demonstrates a pathological area characterized by nodules composed of cells containing abundant brownish granular pigment, identified as lipofuscin. This finding is indicative of cellular aging or specific metabolic conditions.

caption: Adrenocortical adenoma. (A) The adenoma is distinguished from nodular hyperplasia by its solitary, circumscribed nature. The functional status of an adrenocortical adenoma cannot be predicted from its gross or microscopic appearance.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an Adrenocortical adenoma, characterized by a solitary, circumscribed mass. The caption notes that this appearance distinguishes it from nodular hyperplasia and states that functional status cannot be determined from gross or microscopic examination.

caption: Adrenocortical adenoma. (B) Histologic features of an adrenal cortical adenoma. The neoplastic cells are vacuolated because of the presence of intracytoplasmic lipid. There is mild nuclear pleomorphism. Mitotic activity and necrosis are not seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an adrenocortical adenoma, characterized by neoplastic cells with vacuolated cytoplasm due to intracytoplasmic lipid. Mild nuclear pleomorphism is present, but there is no evidence of mitotic activity or necrosis.

caption: A patient with Cushing syndrome. Characteristic features include central obesity, “moon facies,” and abdominal striae. (Reproduced with permis- sion from Lloyd RV, et al: Atlas of nontumor pathology: endocrine diseases. Washington, DC, American Registry of Pathology, 2002.)
summary: The image depicts a patient with Cushing syndrome, characterized by central obesity, moon facies, and abdominal striae.

caption: Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome. Bilateral adrenal hemor- rhage in an infant with overwhelming sepsis, resulting in acute adrenal insuf- ficiency. At autopsy, the adrenal glands were grossly hemorrhagic and shrunken; in this photomicrograph, little residual cortical architecture is discernible.
summary: The image depicts a photomicrograph of an adrenal gland affected by Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome, characterized by extensive hemorrhage and loss of normal cortical architecture due to overwhelming sepsis in an infant.

caption: Autoimmune adrenalitis. In addition to loss of all but a subcap- sular rim of cortical cells, there is an extensive mononuclear cell infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the adrenal gland exhibiting autoimmune adrenalitis. Key features include the near-total destruction of the adrenal cortex, leaving only a thin subcapsular rim of cortical cells, and the presence of a dense, extensive mononuclear cell infiltrate within the glandular tissue.

caption: Adrenal carcinoma. The tumor dwarfs the kidney and com- presses the upper pole. It is largely hemorrhagic and necrotic.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an adrenal gland affected by a carcinoma. The tumor is notably large, described as dwarfing the kidney and compressing its upper pole. Visually, the mass exhibits a reddish appearance with extensive areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

caption: Adrenal carcinoma with marked anaplasia.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an adrenal carcinoma characterized by marked anaplasia. The tissue shows a disorganized architecture with large, pleomorphic cells exhibiting significant nuclear atypia, prominent nucleoli, and high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, indicative of a high-grade malignancy.

caption: Pheochromocytoma. The tumor is enclosed within an attenuated cortex and demonstrates areas of hemorrhage. The comma-shaped residual adrenal gland is seen (lower portion).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a pheochromocytoma, a tumor arising from the adrenal medulla. The tumor is large, encapsulated, and exhibits areas of hemorrhage. A comma-shaped residual adrenal gland is visible at the lower portion of the specimen.

caption: Photomicrograph of pheochromocytoma, demonstrating charac- teristic nests of cells with abundant cytoplasm. Granules containing cate- cholamine are not visible in this preparation. It is not uncommon to find bizarre cells (such as the one in the center of this image), even in pheochro- mocytomas that are benign.
summary: The image is a photomicrograph of a pheochromocytoma, showing characteristic nests of cells with abundant cytoplasm. Granules containing catecholamines are not visible in this preparation. It is not uncommon to find bizarre cells (such as the one in the center of this image), even in pheochromocytomas that are benign.

caption: Woven bone (A) is more cellular and disorganized than lamellar bone
summary: The image displays a histological section of woven bone, characterized by a disorganized arrangement of collagen fibers and a high density of osteocytes within lacunae, contrasting with the organized structure of lamellar bone.

caption: Woven bone (B).
summary: The image displays a histological section of woven bone, characterized by a disorganized arrangement of collagen fibers and the presence of osteocytes within lacunae, indicating immature bone tissue.

caption: Active growth plate with ongoing endochondral ossification. 1, Reserve zone. 2, Zone of proliferation. 3, Zone of hypertrophy. 4, Zone of mineralization. 5, Primary spongiosa.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an active growth plate undergoing endochondral ossification. It illustrates the distinct zones of the growth plate, including the reserve zone, zone of proliferation, zone of hypertrophy, zone of mineralization, and the primary spongiosa, highlighting the cellular changes and matrix maturation involved in bone formation.

caption: Osteoporotic vertebral body (right) shortened by compression fractures compared with a normal vertebral body (left). Note that the osteo- porotic vertebra has a characteristic loss of horizontal trabeculae and thick- ened vertical trabeculae.
summary: The image displays a comparison between a normal vertebral body and an osteoporotic vertebral body. The normal vertebra (left) exhibits a dense trabecular bone structure, while the osteoporotic vertebra (right) shows significant bone loss, characterized by the thinning of horizontal trabeculae and the thickening of vertical trabeculae, resulting in a shortened shape due to compression fractures.

caption: In advanced osteoporosis, both the trabecular bone of the medulla (bottom) and the cortical bone (top) are markedly thinned.
summary: The image displays a histological section of bone tissue, likely stained with H&E, illustrating the structural changes associated with advanced osteoporosis. The caption identifies two distinct regions: the medullary cavity at the bottom and the cortical bone at the top. Both areas exhibit significant thinning of the trabecular bone (medulla) and the cortical bone, respectively, which is the hallmark pathological finding of the condition.

caption: Hyperparathyroidism with osteoclasts boring into the center of the trabecula resembling a train track (dissecting osteitis).
summary: The image displays a histological section of bone tissue exhibiting features of hyperparathyroidism. The key finding is the presence of numerous osteoclasts actively resorbing the bone trabeculae, creating a distinct pattern where the cells appear to be boring into the center of the bone structure, resembling a train track. This process is identified as dissecting osteitis.

caption: Resected rib, harboring an expansile brown tumor adjacent to the costal cartilage.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a resected rib. The specimen features a large, expansile, brown-colored mass located adjacent to the costal cartilage, identified as a brown tumor.

caption: Mosaic pattern of lamellar bone pathognomonic of Paget disease.
summary: The image displays a histological section of bone tissue exhibiting a mosaic pattern of lamellar bone, which is a characteristic finding indicative of Paget disease.

caption: Femoral head with a subchondral, wedge-shaped pale yellow area of osteonecrosis (arrow). The space between the overlying articular cartilage and bone is caused by trabecular compression fractures without repair.
summary: The image displays a cross-section of a femoral head exhibiting osteonecrosis. A distinct, wedge-shaped, pale yellow area is visible within the subchondral bone, indicating necrotic tissue. The caption notes that the space between this necrotic area and the overlying articular cartilage is formed by trabecular compression fractures that have not undergone repair.

caption: Resected femur in a person with draining osteomyelitis. The drainage tract in the subperiosteal shell of viable new bone (involucrum) shows the inner native necrotic cortex (sequestrum).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a resected femur affected by chronic osteomyelitis. The specimen reveals a subperiosteal shell of viable new bone, known as an involucrum, which encases a central cavity containing the inner native necrotic bone cortex, referred to as a sequestrum. A drainage tract is visible within the involucrum.

caption: Osteoid osteoma composed of haphazardly interconnecting tra- beculae of woven bone that are rimmed by prominent osteoblasts. The intertrabecular spaces are filled by vascularized loose connective tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an osteoid osteoma, characterized by irregular trabeculae of woven bone surrounded by active osteoblasts, with the spaces between trabeculae containing vascularized loose connective tissue.

caption: Distal femoral osteosarcoma with prominent bone formation extending into the soft tissues. The periosteum, which has been lifted, has laid down a triangular shell of reactive bone known as a Codman triangle (arrow).
summary: The image displays a radiograph of a distal femur exhibiting an osteosarcoma. The tumor is characterized by prominent bone formation extending into the surrounding soft tissues. A key diagnostic feature visible is a Codman triangle, which is a triangular shell of reactive bone formed by the periosteum being lifted by the tumor.

caption: Osteosarcoma of the proximal tibia. The tan-white tumor fills most of the medullary cavity of the metaphysis and proximal diaphysis. It has infiltrated through the cortex, lifted the periosteum, and formed soft tissue masses on both sides of the bone.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a proximal tibia affected by osteosarcoma. The tan-white tumor mass is seen occupying the medullary cavity of the metaphysis and extending into the proximal diaphysis. The tumor has breached the cortical bone and lifted the periosteum, resulting in the formation of soft tissue masses on both sides of the bone.

caption: Fine, lacelike pattern of neoplastic bone produced by anaplastic malignant tumor cells in an osteosarcoma. Note the abnormal mitotic figure (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of an osteosarcoma, characterized by a fine, lace-like pattern of neoplastic bone produced by anaplastic malignant tumor cells. An abnormal mitotic figure is indicated by an arrow, highlighting the malignant nature of the cells.

caption: Osteochondroma. (B) The cartilage cap has the histologic appearance of disorganized growth plate–like cartilage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an osteochondroma, characterized by a disorganized cartilage cap resembling a growth plate, situated above a bony structure.

caption: Osteochondroma. (A) Radiograph of an osteochondroma arising from the distal femur (arrow).
summary: The image is a radiograph showing an osteochondroma, a benign bone tumor, arising from the distal femur. The lesion appears as an exophytic bony growth with continuity of the medullary cavity, characteristic of osteochondroma.

caption: Enchondroma of the proximal phalanx. The radiolucent nodule of cartilage with central calcification thins but does not penetrate the cortex.
summary: The image displays an X-ray of a hand, specifically focusing on the proximal phalanx of a finger. It shows a radiolucent nodule with central calcification, characteristic of an enchondroma, which thins but does not penetrate the cortex of the bone.

caption: Enchondroma composed of a nodule of hyaline cartilage encased by a thin layer of reactive bone.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an enchondroma, characterized by a nodule of hyaline cartilage surrounded by a thin layer of reactive bone. The cartilage appears as a pale, basophilic area with chondrocytes in lacunae, while the reactive bone is stained pink and shows trabecular structures.

caption: Chondrosarcoma. (A) Nodules of hyaline and myxoid cartilage permeating throughout the medullary cavity, growing through the cortex, and forming a relatively well-circumscribed soft tissue mass.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a chondrosarcoma, characterized by nodules of hyaline and myxoid cartilage that have permeated the medullary cavity, breached the cortex, and formed a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass.

caption: Chondrosarcoma. (B) Conventional chondrosarcoma entraps native lamellar bone as a confluent mass of cartilage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a conventional chondrosarcoma. It shows a confluent mass of cartilage tissue that is entrapping native lamellar bone, which appears as bright pink structures.

caption: Ewing sarcoma composed of sheets of small round cells with small amounts of clear cytoplasm.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Ewing sarcoma, characterized by sheets of small round cells with scanty clear cytoplasm.

caption: Radiographically, giant cell tumor of the proximal fibula is pre- dominantly lytic, expansile with destruction of the cortex. A pathologic fracture is also present.
summary: The image is a radiograph of the left knee showing a giant cell tumor of the proximal fibula. The tumor is predominantly lytic, expansile, and has caused destruction of the cortex. Additionally, a pathologic fracture is present.

caption: Giant cell tumor illustrating an abundance of multinucleated giant cells with background mononuclear stromal cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Giant Cell Tumor, characterized by a dense population of multinucleated giant cells interspersed with a background of mononuclear stromal cells.

caption: (B) Axial magnetic resonance image demonstrating characteristic fluid-fluid levels (arrow).
summary: The image is an axial magnetic resonance image (MRI) of a limb, likely a lower extremity, displaying a cross-section of soft tissue. The image highlights a distinct lesion characterized by a 'fluid-fluid level,' indicated by an arrow, which appears as a bright, hyperintense area within the darker surrounding tissue.

caption: (A) Coronal computed axial tomography scan showing eccentric aneurysmal bone cyst of tibia. The soft tissue component is delineated by a thin rim of reactive subperiosteal bone.
summary: The image is a coronal computed axial tomography (CT) scan of the knee, specifically showing an eccentric aneurysmal bone cyst located in the tibia. The soft tissue component of the cyst is delineated by a thin rim of reactive subperiosteal bone.

caption: Aneurysmal bone cyst with blood-filled cystic space surrounded by a fibrous wall containing proliferating fibroblasts, reactive woven bone, and osteoclast-type giant cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an aneurysmal bone cyst, characterized by a large, blood-filled cystic space. This space is surrounded by a fibrous wall composed of proliferating fibroblasts, reactive woven bone, and osteoclast-type giant cells.

caption: Nonossifying fibroma of the distal tibia metaphysis producing an eccentric lobulated radiolucency surrounded by a sclerotic margin.
summary: The image is an X-ray of the distal tibia showing a nonossifying fibroma, characterized by an eccentric, lobulated radiolucency with a sclerotic margin in the metaphysis.

caption: Fibrous dysplasia composed of curvilinear trabeculae of woven bone that lack conspicuous osteoblastic rimming and arise in a background of fibrous tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of fibrous dysplasia, characterized by irregular, curvilinear trabeculae of woven bone embedded within a cellular fibrous stroma. The bone trabeculae are notably devoid of osteoblastic rimming.

caption: Storiform pattern created by benign spindle cells with scattered osteoclast-type giant cells characteristic of a fibrous cortical defect.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing a storiform pattern formed by benign spindle cells, interspersed with scattered osteoclast-type giant cells, which is characteristic of a fibrous cortical defect.

caption: Osteoarthritis. (A) Histologic demonstration of the characteristic fibrillation of the articular cartilage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of articular cartilage affected by osteoarthritis, characterized by fibrillation and surface irregularities.

caption: Osteoarthritis. (B) Eburnated articular surface exposing subchondral bone (1), subchondral cyst (2), and residual articular cartilage (3).
summary: The image displays a histological section of an osteoarthritic joint, specifically highlighting the articular surface. Key pathological features include eburnation of the articular surface, indicating severe cartilage loss and exposure of the underlying subchondral bone. Additionally, a subchondral cyst is visible within the bone, and a small amount of residual articular cartilage remains.

caption: Severe osteoarthritis of the hip. The joint space is narrowed, and there is subchondral sclerosis with scattered oval radiolucent cysts and peripheral osteophyte lipping (arrows).
summary: The image is an X-ray of a hip joint demonstrating severe osteoarthritis. Key radiographic features include significant narrowing of the joint space, subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density beneath the cartilage), the presence of multiple subchondral cysts (appearing as radiolucent or dark oval areas), and the formation of osteophytes (bony outgrowths) at the joint margins, indicated by arrows.

caption: Rheumatoid nodule composed of central necrosis rimmed by palisaded histiocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a rheumatoid nodule, characterized by a central area of necrosis surrounded by a rim of palisaded histiocytes.

caption: Rheumatoid arthritis of the hand. Characteristic features include diffuse osteopenia, marked loss of the joint spaces of the carpal, metacarpal, phalangeal, and interphalangeal joints, periarticular bony erosions, and ulnar drift of the fingers.
summary: The image displays an X-ray of a hand exhibiting characteristic features of rheumatoid arthritis, including diffuse osteopenia, marked loss of joint spaces in the carpal, metacarpal, phalangeal, and interphalangeal joints, periarticular bony erosions, and ulnar drift of the fingers.

caption: Tenosynovial giant cell tumor, diffuse type. (A) Excised synovium with fronds and nodules typical of pigmented villonodular synovitis.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an excised synovium, identified as a Tenosynovial giant cell tumor, diffuse type. The tissue exhibits characteristic fronds and nodules, consistent with the presentation of pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS).

caption: Tenosynovial giant cell tumor, diffuse type. (B) Sheets of proliferating cells in tenosynovial giant cell tumor bulging the synovial lining.
summary: The image shows a histological section of a tenosynovial giant cell tumor, diffuse type, characterized by sheets of proliferating cells bulging the synovial lining.

caption: Liposarcoma. (A) The well-differentiated subtype consists of mature adipocytes and scattered spindle cells with hyperchromatic nuclei.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a well-differentiated liposarcoma, characterized by the presence of mature adipocytes interspersed with scattered spindle cells that exhibit hyperchromatic nuclei.

caption: Liposarcoma. (B) Myxoid liposarcoma with abundant ground substance and a rich capillary network in which there are scattered immature adipocytes and more primitive round-to- stellate cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of myxoid liposarcoma, characterized by an abundant myxoid stroma (ground substance) and a prominent, delicate capillary network. Scattered within this matrix are immature adipocytes and primitive round-to-stellate cells.

caption: Nodular fasciitis with plump, randomly oriented spindle cells surrounded by myxoid stroma. Note the mitotic activity (arrowheads).
summary: The image displays a histological section of Nodular Fasciitis, characterized by a proliferation of plump, randomly oriented spindle cells embedded within a myxoid stroma. The caption highlights the presence of mitotic activity, indicated by arrowheads, which is a feature of this reactive lesion.

caption: Deep fibromatosis infiltrating between skeletal muscle cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section showing deep fibromatosis, characterized by a proliferation of spindle cells infiltrating between skeletal muscle fibers. The tissue architecture reveals the tumor cells disrupting the normal muscle structure.

caption: Synovial sarcoma showing the classic biphasic spindle cell and glandlike histologic appearance.
summary: The image displays a histological section of synovial sarcoma, characterized by a classic biphasic pattern. This pattern consists of intermixed spindle cells and gland-like structures, which is a hallmark of this specific soft tissue tumor.

caption: Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma showing anaplastic spin- dled to polygonal cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, characterized by a disorganized proliferation of anaplastic cells with spindle to polygonal shapes.

caption: Pathologic changes in peripheral neuropathies. (A) Regeneration after segmental demyelination. Teased fiber preparations allow for examination of individual axons of peripheral nerves. A normal axon (left) with a long thick dark myelin internode flanked by nodes of Ranvier (arrows; compare with Fig. 22.1A). All myelin internodes along his axon would be expected to be of similar length and width. The right axon, by contrast, shows a segment surrounded by a series of thinly myelinated internodes of uneven length flanked on both ends by normal thicker myelin internodes (arrows mark nodes of Ranvier; compare with Fig. 22.1C). (B–C) Vasculitic neuropathy. In
summary: The image illustrates pathologic changes in peripheral neuropathies, specifically focusing on regeneration after segmental demyelination. It shows a normal axon with a long thick dark myelin internode flanked by nodes of Ranvier, and a regenerated axon with a series of thinly myelinated internodes of uneven length, also flanked by normal thicker myelin internodes. The caption also mentions vasculitic neuropathy, though the specific details for this condition are not provided in the image.

caption: Pathologic changes in peripheral neuropathies. (B), the perineurial connective tissue contains an inflammatory infiltrate around small blood vessels that has obliterated a vessel. In
summary: The image displays a histological section of peripheral nerve tissue, specifically highlighting perineurial connective tissue. The caption identifies this as a case of peripheral neuropathy, characterized by an inflammatory infiltrate surrounding small blood vessels within the perineurium, leading to the obliteration of the vessel lumen.

caption: Pathologic changes in peripheral neuropathies. (C), a special stain that colors myelinated axons dark blue hue shows that the nerve fascicle in the upper portion of this field (asterisk) has lost almost all of its large myelinated axons, in contrast with the other fascicle shown. Such interfascicular variation in axonal density is often seen in neuropathies resulting from vascular injury.
summary: The image displays a histological section of peripheral nerves stained with a special stain (likely Luxol Fast Blue) to visualize myelinated axons. It illustrates pathologic changes in peripheral neuropathies, specifically highlighting a nerve fascicle (marked with an asterisk) that has lost almost all of its large myelinated axons, contrasting with an adjacent fascicle that retains a higher density of axons. This variation in axonal density between fascicles is characteristic of neuropathies resulting from vascular injury.

caption: The dystrophin-glycoprotein complex (DGC). This complex of glycoproteins serves to couple the cell membrane (the sarcolemma) to extracellular matrix proteins such as laminin-2 and the intracellular cytoskel- eton. A key set of connections is made by dystrophin, a scaffolding protein that tethers the myofibrillar cytoskeleton to the transmembrane dystrogly- cans and sarcoglycans, and also binds signaling complexes containing dystro- brevin, syntrophin, neuronal nitric oxide synthetase (nNOS), and caveolin. Mutations in dystrophin are associated with X-linked Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies; mutations in caveolin and the sarcoglycan proteins with autosomal limb-girdle muscular dystrophies; and mutations in α2- laminin (merosin) with a form of congenital muscular dystrophy.
summary: The image illustrates the dystrophin-glycoprotein complex (DGC), which connects the sarcolemma to extracellular matrix proteins like laminin-2 and the intracellular cytoskeleton. Dystrophin acts as a scaffolding protein, linking the myofibrillar cytoskeleton to transmembrane dystroglycans and sarcoglycans, while also binding signaling complexes including dystrobrevin, syntrophin, neuronal nitric oxide synthetase (nNOS), and caveolin. Mutations in these proteins are associated with various forms of muscular dystrophy, including X-linked Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies, autosomal limb-girdle muscular dystrophies, and congenital muscular dystrophy.

caption: Patterns of skeletal muscle injury. (C) Neurogenic changes. (C1) This diagrammatic representation of four normal motor units shows a checkerboard-type admixture of light and dark stained fibers of opposite type. (C2) Damage to innervating axons leads to a loss of trophic input and the atrophy of myofibers. (C3) Reinnervation of myofibers can result in a switch in fiber type and segregation of fibers of like type. As illustrated here, reinnervation is also often associated with an increase in motor unit size, with more myofibers innervated by an individual axon. (C4) Normal muscle has a checkerboard distribution of type I (light) and type II (dark) fibers on this ATPase reaction (pH9.4), corresponding to findings in (A). (C5) Clustered flattened “angulated” atrophic fibers (grouped atrophy) are a typical finding associated with disrupted innervation. (C6) With ongoing denervation and reinnervation, large clusters of fibers appear that all share the same fiber type (fiber type grouping).
summary: The image illustrates the progression of neurogenic changes in skeletal muscle, starting from a normal checkerboard pattern of light (type I) and dark (type II) fibers, through axonal damage leading to fiber atrophy, reinnervation causing fiber type switching and motor unit enlargement, and culminating in clustered atrophic fibers and fiber type grouping.

caption: Patterns of skeletal muscle injury. (A) Normal skeletal muscle has relatively uniform polygonal myofibers with peripherally placed nuclei that are tightly packed together into fascicles separated by scant connective tissue. A perimysial interfascicular septum containing a blood vessel is present (top center).
summary: The image displays a histological section of normal skeletal muscle tissue. It features polygonal myofibers with uniform appearance and peripherally located nuclei. These fibers are organized into fascicles, which are separated by minimal connective tissue. A perimysial interfascicular septum containing a blood vessel is visible at the top center of the section.

caption: Patterns of skeletal muscle injury. (B) Myopathic conditions often are associated with segmental necrosis and regeneration of individual myofibers. Necrotic cells (B1–B3) are infiltrated by variable numbers of inflammatory cells. Regenerative myofibers (B4, arrow) are characterized by cytoplasmic basophilia and enlarged nucleoli (not visible at this power).
summary: The image illustrates patterns of skeletal muscle injury, specifically highlighting myopathic conditions characterized by segmental necrosis and regeneration of individual myofibers. Necrotic cells are infiltrated by inflammatory cells, while regenerative myofibers exhibit cytoplasmic basophilia and enlarged nucleoli.

caption: Patterns of neuronal injury. (C) Collection of microglial cells forming a poorly defined nodule, a common finding in viral infections.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue, specifically highlighting a collection of microglial cells forming a poorly defined nodule, which is a common pathological finding associated with viral infections.

caption: Patterns of neuronal injury. (A) Acute hypoxic-ischemic injury in the cerebral cortex. The cell bodies are shrunken and eosinophilic (“red neurons”), and the nuclei are pyknotic.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the cerebral cortex exhibiting acute hypoxic-ischemic injury. The key pathological features visible are shrunken, eosinophilic (red) neurons and pyknotic nuclei, which are characteristic of this type of neuronal damage.

caption: Patterns of neuronal injury. (B) Reactive astrocytes, with eosinophilic cytoplasm and multiple radiating processes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue, specifically highlighting reactive astrocytes. These cells are characterized by their eosinophilic (pink-staining) cytoplasm and multiple radiating processes, indicating a response to injury or pathology.

caption: Cerebral edema. The surfaces of the gyri are flattened as a result of compression of the expanding brain by the dura mater and inner surface of the skull. Such changes are associated with a dangerous increase in intra- cranial pressure.
summary: The image depicts cerebral edema, characterized by the flattening of the gyri due to compression from the expanding brain against the dura mater and skull. This condition is associated with a dangerous increase in intracranial pressure.

caption: Hydrocephalus. Dilated lateral ventricles seen in a coronal section through the mid-thalamus.
summary: The image displays a coronal section of a brain, specifically through the mid-thalamus, illustrating the condition of hydrocephalus. The primary visual feature is the significant dilation of the lateral ventricles, which appear as large, dark, fluid-filled spaces, contrasting with the compressed surrounding brain tissue.

caption: Duret hemorrhage. As mass effect displaces the brain downward, there is disruption of the vessels that enter the pons along the midline, leading to hemorrhage.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brainstem, specifically the pons, exhibiting a Duret hemorrhage. This condition is characterized by a large, dark, linear hemorrhage located along the midline of the pons, resulting from the downward displacement of the brain caused by mass effect.

caption: Cerebral infarction. (A) Infiltration of a cerebral infarction by neutrophils begins at the edges of the lesion, where the vascular supply is intact.
summary: The image identifies Fig. 23.6 as a cerebral infarction, depicting the infiltration by neutrophils originating from the margins of the lesion where the vascular supply remains functional.

caption: Cerebral infarction. (B) By day 10, an area of infarction shows the presence of macrophages and surrounding reactive gliosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue from day 10 post-cerebral infarction. It reveals an area of infarction characterized by the presence of macrophages and surrounding reactive gliosis.

caption: Cerebral infarction. (C) Old intracortical infarcts are seen as areas of tissue loss and residual gliosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue, specifically showing an old intracortical infarct. The area is characterized by tissue loss and the presence of residual gliosis, indicated by the pinkish, scarred appearance of the tissue.

caption: Cerebral hemorrhage. Massive hypertensive hemorrhage ruptur- ing into a lateral ventricle.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain exhibiting a massive cerebral hemorrhage, characterized by a large, dark, necrotic area. The caption identifies this as a hypertensive hemorrhage that has ruptured and extended into the lateral ventricle.

caption: Saccular aneurysms. (B) The circle of Willis is dissected to show a large aneurysm.
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical specimen of the Circle of Willis, a major arterial ring at the base of the brain. The specimen has been dissected to reveal a large, dark red, saccular aneurysm arising from one of the vessels within the circle.

caption: Saccular aneurysms. (C) Section through a saccular aneurysm showing the hyalinized fibrous vessel wall. Hematoxylin-eosin stain.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a saccular aneurysm, characterized by a dilated vessel wall. The wall exhibits hyalinization, indicating fibrous tissue changes, and is stained with Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E).

caption: Saccular aneurysms. (A) View of the base of the brain, dissected to show the circle of Willis with an aneurysm of the anterior cerebral artery (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical view of the base of the brain, specifically the Circle of Willis. It highlights a saccular aneurysm located on the anterior cerebral artery, indicated by an arrow.

caption: Arteriovenous malformation.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain, showcasing a large, dark, and irregularly shaped mass indicative of an arteriovenous malformation (AVM). The AVM appears as a tangled network of blood vessels, contrasting with the surrounding normal brain tissue.

caption: Myelomeningocele. Both meninges and spinal cord parenchyma are included in the cystlike structure visible just above the buttocks.
summary: The image displays a clinical photograph of a newborn's lower back, specifically showing a myelomeningocele. This condition is characterized by a cyst-like sac protruding from the spine, located just above the buttocks. The sac contains both meningeal tissue and spinal cord parenchyma, which are visible within the structure.

caption: Perinatal brain injury. This specimen from a patient with periven- tricular leukomalacia contains a central focus of white matter necrosis with a peripheral rim of mineralized axonal processes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a perinatal brain, specifically illustrating periventricular leukomalacia (PVL). The central area shows necrosis of the white matter, characterized by a loss of normal tissue architecture. Surrounding this necrotic core is a distinct peripheral rim composed of mineralized axonal processes, indicating a specific pathological response to the injury.

caption: Cryptococcal infection. (A) Whole-brain section showing the numerous areas of tissue destruction associated with the spread of organisms in the perivascular spaces.
summary: The image displays a whole-brain section stained with H&E, illustrating the pathological effects of a Cryptococcal infection. The section reveals extensive tissue destruction and the presence of organisms within the perivascular spaces, indicating the spread of the infection throughout the brain tissue.

caption: Cryptococcal infection. (B) At higher magnification, it is possible to see the cryptococci in the lesions.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tissue exhibiting a Cryptococcal infection. At higher magnification, the image reveals a granulomatous lesion containing numerous encapsulated yeast-like organisms (cryptococci) within a macrophage or giant cell, surrounded by a rim of inflammatory cells.

caption: Toxoplasma infection. (A) Abscesses are present in the putamen and thalamus.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue, specifically highlighting the putamen and thalamus, which exhibit abscesses indicative of a Toxoplasma infection.

caption: Toxoplasma infection. (B) Free tachyzoites are demonstrated by immunohistochemical staining. Inset, Bradyzoites are present as a pseudocyst, again highlighted by immunohistochemical staining.
summary: The image displays a histological section demonstrating Toxoplasma infection, with immunohistochemical staining highlighting free tachyzoites and a pseudocyst containing bradyzoites.

caption: Prion disease. (A) Histologic features of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) include spongiform change in the cerebral cortex. Inset, High magnifica- tion of a neuron with vacuoles.
summary: The image displays histological features of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), a prion disease, characterized by spongiform change in the cerebral cortex. An inset provides a high-magnification view of a neuron exhibiting vacuoles.

caption: Prion disease. (B) Variant CJD (vCJD) is characterized by amyloid plaques (inset) that sit in the regions of greatest spongiform change.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue affected by a prion disease, specifically Variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (vCJD). The tissue exhibits characteristic spongiform changes, appearing as a pink background with numerous small, empty vacuoles. An inset highlights the presence of amyloid plaques, which are distinct, darker, irregularly shaped deposits located within the areas of spongiform degeneration.

caption: Alzheimer disease. (A) Plaques (arrow) contain a central core of amyloid and a surrounding region of dystrophic neurites (Bielschowsky stain).
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue affected by Alzheimer's disease, specifically highlighting the presence of amyloid plaques. These plaques are stained using the Bielschowsky method, which reveals a central core of amyloid surrounded by dystrophic neurites.

caption: Alzheimer disease. (B) Immunohistochemical stain for Aβ. Peptide is present in the core of the plaques as well as in the surrounding region.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue affected by Alzheimer's disease, specifically showing immunohistochemical staining for amyloid-beta (Aβ). The brown staining highlights the presence of Aβ peptide within the core of the plaques and in the surrounding region, indicating amyloid deposition characteristic of the disease.

caption: Alzheimer disease. (C) Neurons containing tangles stained with an antibody specific for tau.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue affected by Alzheimer's disease. It features neurons containing neurofibrillary tangles, which are stained brown using an antibody specific for the protein tau.

caption: Parkinson disease. (B) Depigmented substantia nigra in idiopathic Parkinson disease.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a brain, specifically highlighting the substantia nigra. The caption identifies this as a case of idiopathic Parkinson disease, characterized by the visible depigmentation of the substantia nigra, which appears as a dark, depigmented area compared to the surrounding tissue.

caption: Parkinson disease. (A) Normal substantia nigra.
summary: The image displays a gross anatomical section of the brain, specifically the midbrain, showing the substantia nigra. The caption identifies this as a normal substantia nigra in the context of Parkinson disease, implying a comparison with pathological states where this region typically shows degeneration.

caption: Parkinson disease. (C) Lewy body in a neuron from the substantia nigra stains pink.
summary: The image displays a neuron from the substantia nigra in a case of Parkinson disease, featuring a prominent pink-staining Lewy body within the cytoplasm, indicative of the pathological hallmark of the disease.

caption: Huntington disease. Normal hemisphere (left) compared with a hemisphere with Huntington disease (right) showing atrophy of the striatum and ventricular dilation. Inset, An intranuclear inclusion in a cortical neuron is strongly immunoreactive for ubiquitin. (Gross photo courtesy of Dr. Vonsattel, Columbia University, New York, New York.)
summary: The image displays a gross pathology comparison of two brain hemispheres: a normal hemisphere on the left and a hemisphere affected by Huntington disease on the right. The diseased hemisphere exhibits significant atrophy of the striatum and dilation of the ventricles. An inset shows an intranuclear inclusion in a cortical neuron that is strongly immunoreactive for ubiquitin.

caption: In oligodendroglioma, tumor cells have round nuclei, often with a clear cytoplasmic halo. Blood vessels in the background are thin and can form an interlacing pattern.
